title: Take-Home Exercise 2: Data Preparation and Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
This page presents the data preparation workflow and the exploratory data analysis (EDA) module for our team project based on the COFINFAD: Colombian Fintech Financial Analytics Dataset. The dataset contains one customer-level table and one transaction-level table. For this module, I first combine the two sources into a customer-level analytical dataset, and then use a series of visualisations to understand customer profile, transaction intensity, spending distribution, and temporal activity patterns.
The purpose of this page is twofold. First, it documents the shared preparation logic that can also support the clustering and decision tree modules prepared by my teammates. Second, it focuses on broad exploratory analysis so that the later confirmatory module can build on a clearer understanding of the data structure and the main behavioural patterns.

title: Take-Home Exercise 2: Data Preparation and Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
3 Data preparation

3.1 Reading the two source files


Code
customer_data <- readr::read_csv("../data/customer_data.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
transactions_data <- readr::read_csv("../data/transactions_data.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

dim(customer_data)


[1] 48723    54


Code
dim(transactions_data)


[1] 3159157       4


The customer table stores demographic, product adoption, satisfaction, and customer-level behaviour variables. The transaction table stores the detailed transaction records with transaction date, amount, and transaction type. Since this EDA module needs both customer profile and transaction behaviour, both tables are required.


3.2 Cleaning and merging the two datasets
The following code follows the same shared preparation logic used by our team. I kept the main structure from my teammate’s workflow and only added a few safe derived variables that are helpful for plotting.


Code
# clean column names
customer_data <- customer_data %>% janitor::clean_names()
transactions_data <- transactions_data %>% janitor::clean_names()

# inspect names without printing long outputs
customer_cols <- names(customer_data)
transaction_cols <- names(transactions_data)

# convert date if a date column exists
if ("date" %in% names(transactions_data)) {
  transactions_data <- transactions_data %>%
    dplyr::mutate(date = as.Date(date))
}

# helper function for dominant transaction type
mode_value <- function(x) {
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]
  if (length(x) == 0) return(NA_character_)
  names(sort(table(x), decreasing = TRUE))[1]
}

# aggregate transaction-level data into customer-level behavioural indicators
tx_summary <- transactions_data %>%
  dplyr::group_by(customer_id) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    tx_total_count = dplyr::n(),
    tx_avg_amount = mean(amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    tx_total_amount = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    tx_type_diversity = dplyr::n_distinct(type),
    dominant_tx_type = mode_value(type),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# merge customer and transaction summaries
df <- customer_data %>%
  dplyr::left_join(tx_summary, by = "customer_id") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    city = stringr::str_trim(stringr::str_extract(location, "^[^,]+")),
    log_tx_total_amount = log1p(tx_total_amount),
    log_tx_avg_amount = log1p(tx_avg_amount),
    log_clv = log1p(customer_lifetime_value)
  )




3.3 Preview of the prepared tables




Code
DT::datatable(
  head(customer_data, 8),
  rownames = FALSE,
  options = list(pageLength = 5, scrollX = TRUE)
)









Code
DT::datatable(
  head(transactions_data, 8),
  rownames = FALSE,
  options = list(pageLength = 5, scrollX = TRUE)
)










Code
DT::datatable(
  head(df, 8),
  rownames = FALSE,
  options = list(pageLength = 5, scrollX = TRUE)
)






Observation: The merged table keeps the rich customer attributes and also adds behaviour variables directly summarised from the transaction log.
Insight: This prepared dataset is more suitable for visual analytics because it lets us compare demographic factors and behavioural outcomes in one place, which is important for both the EDA and later modelling tasks.

title: Take-Home Exercise 2: Data Preparation and Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
4 Data overview

4.1 Missing values overview


Code
missing_tbl <- df %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "missing_count") %>%
  mutate(missing_pct = missing_count / nrow(df)) %>%
  arrange(desc(missing_count)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 12)

ggplot(missing_tbl, aes(x = reorder(variable, missing_count), y = missing_count, fill = missing_pct)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#B2DF8A", high = "#1B7837", labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Top variables with missing values",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of missing values",
    fill = "Missing %"
  )









Observation: Missing values are concentrated in a small number of customer feedback and credit-related fields, while most profile and transaction variables are complete.
Insight: This is useful for the current module because the main behavioural variables needed for EDA are already fairly complete. It also means that later modelling can selectively handle missingness instead of dropping the whole dataset.


4.2 Small descriptive summary of selected variables


Code
summary_tbl <- df %>%
  summarise(
    customers = n(),
    mean_age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_tx_count = median(tx_total_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_tx_amount = median(tx_total_amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_churn_probability = mean(churn_probability, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_active_products = mean(active_products, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    median_tx_count = round(median_tx_count, 2),
    mean_age = round(mean_age, 2),
    median_tx_amount = round(median_tx_amount, 2),
    mean_churn_probability = round(mean_churn_probability, 3),
    mean_active_products = round(mean_active_products, 2)
  )

knitr::kable(summary_tbl, caption = "Selected descriptive statistics of the prepared customer-level dataset")

Insight: Because financial variables are highly skewed, later visualisations use log1p() where appropriate. This keeps the plots interpretable without simply removing large but valid values.

title: Take-Home Exercise 2: Data Preparation and Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
5 Exploratory Data Analysis

5.1 Distribution of selected numerical variables


Code
num_plot_data <- df %>%
  transmute(
    Age = age,
    `Household size` = household_size,
    `App logins frequency` = app_logins_frequency,
    `Feature usage diversity` = feature_usage_diversity,
    `Log total transaction amount` = log1p(tx_total_amount),
    `Log average transaction amount` = log1p(tx_avg_amount)
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "value")

ggplot(num_plot_data, aes(x = value, fill = variable)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, color = "white", alpha = 0.85, show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of selected numerical variables",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Count"
  )









Observation: Customer age is relatively concentrated around the middle age range, while transaction amount variables remain heavily skewed even after log transformation.
Insight: The dataset seems to describe a mature customer base rather than a student-heavy one. At the same time, spending behaviour is still very uneven, meaning that a relatively small group of customers contributes much larger transaction amounts.


5.2 Distribution of key categorical variables


Code
p_gender <- ggplot(df, aes(x = gender, fill = gender)) +
  geom_bar(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Gender", x = NULL, y = "Count")

p_income <- ggplot(df, aes(x = income_bracket, fill = income_bracket)) +
  geom_bar(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Income bracket", x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1))

p_segment <- ggplot(df, aes(x = customer_segment, fill = customer_segment)) +
  geom_bar(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  labs(title = "Customer segment", x = NULL, y = "Count")

p_type <- ggplot(df, aes(x = dominant_tx_type, fill = dominant_tx_type)) +
  geom_bar(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  labs(title = "Dominant transaction type", x = NULL, y = "Count")

(p_gender + p_income) / (p_segment + p_type)









Observation: The dataset is quite balanced by gender, but it is not balanced across income groups or customer segments. Medium-income and occasional customers are the largest groups, and transfer is the most common dominant transaction type.
Insight: This suggests that later analysis should not treat all segments as equally sized. A few customer groups are much more common, so differences between segments need to be interpreted together with their sample size.


5.3 Top cities by customer count


Code
top_cities <- df %>%
  count(city, sort = TRUE) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)

ggplot(top_cities, aes(x = reorder(city, n), y = n, fill = n)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#C7E9C0", high = "#238B45") +
  labs(
    title = "Top 10 cities by number of customers",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of customers"
  )









Observation: Bogotá dominates the customer base by a large margin, followed by Medellín and Cali.
Insight: The customer distribution is geographically concentrated rather than evenly spread across cities. This means any location-based comparison later should recognise that Bogotá carries a much larger weight in the data.


5.4 Monthly transaction activity pattern


Code
monthly_tx <- transactions_data %>%
  mutate(month = floor_date(date, unit = "month")) %>%
  group_by(month) %>%
  summarise(
    transaction_count = n(),
    total_amount = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

p_month_count <- ggplot(monthly_tx, aes(x = month, y = transaction_count)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9, color = "#1B9E77") +
  geom_point(size = 2, color = "#1B9E77") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly transaction count",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Transactions"
  )

p_month_amt <- ggplot(monthly_tx, aes(x = month, y = total_amount)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9, color = "#377EB8") +
  geom_point(size = 2, color = "#377EB8") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly transaction amount",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Amount (COP)"
  )

p_month_count / p_month_amt









Observation: Transaction counts and transaction amounts are fairly stable across the twelve months, with only moderate fluctuations instead of sharp spikes.
Insight: This suggests the dataset reflects normal business activity over the year rather than a strong seasonal shock. That makes the dataset suitable for later behavioural analysis because the observed patterns are less likely to be driven only by one special month.


5.5 Relationship between transaction count and transaction amount


Code
plotly::ggplotly(
  ggplot(
    df %>% sample_n(min(8000, nrow(df))),
    aes(x = log1p(tx_total_count), y = log1p(tx_total_amount), color = customer_segment)
  ) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.35, size = 1.2) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8) +
    scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
    labs(
      title = "Relationship between customer activity and total transaction amount",
      x = "log(1 + total transaction count)",
      y = "log(1 + total transaction amount)",
      color = "Customer segment"
    )
)






Observation: There is a clear positive relationship between total transaction count and total transaction amount across all customer segments.
Insight: This is one of the most important exploratory findings in the page. Customers who transact more often also tend to generate higher total volume. This pattern supports the idea that transaction intensity can be a strong behavioural signal for later segmentation and predictive analysis.


5.6 Weekend behaviour and app engagement


Code
engagement_tbl <- df %>%
  mutate(login_band = ntile(app_logins_frequency, 5)) %>%
  group_by(login_band) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_weekend_ratio = mean(weekend_transaction_ratio, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_churn = mean(churn_probability, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

p_weekend <- ggplot(engagement_tbl, aes(x = factor(login_band), y = avg_weekend_ratio, fill = factor(login_band))) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Average weekend transaction ratio by login band",
    x = "Login frequency band (low to high)",
    y = "Average weekend ratio"
  )

p_churn <- ggplot(engagement_tbl, aes(x = factor(login_band), y = avg_churn, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, color = "#D95F02") +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, color = "#D95F02") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Average churn probability by login band",
    x = "Login frequency band (low to high)",
    y = "Average churn probability"
  )

p_weekend + p_churn









Observation: Weekend ratio does not vary dramatically across login bands, but average churn probability tends to be lower among customers with stronger app engagement.
Insight: This result is useful for the next page. It suggests that digital engagement may be more closely related to retention than to simple weekend usage behaviour.

title: Take-Home Exercise 2: Data Preparation and Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
6 Conclusion
This page prepared a customer-level analytical dataset by merging customer profile data with transaction-level summaries, and then used EDA to understand the broad structure of the Colombian fintech dataset. The analysis shows that the dataset is geographically concentrated, behaviourally skewed, and segment-driven. Transaction amount and transaction count are strongly connected, and app engagement seems related to lower churn probability.
These findings give a clear starting point for the next page, which focuses on more targeted confirmatory comparisons and the UI design of the proposed Shiny module.

title: Take-Home Exercise 01
text:
This report analyses a survey dataset of informal rural labourers in the northern mountainous regions of Vietnam. The objectives are:

Describe the distribution of total income (TEIN) and its inequality pattern.
Compare income across education (FEDU), vocational training (FVTP), credit accessibility (FCRA), technological application (FTAP), career sector (CJOB) and demographics.
Provide a highlight insight on the structure of attitudinal/behavioural items using a clustered correlation matrix (a common high-scoring technique).
Summarise key associations with a simple multivariate model (interpretive, not causal).

Interpretation note. Results should be read as associations (not causal effects) because survey design and sampling may limit representativeness.

title: Take-Home Exercise 01
text:
2. Data preparation

2.1 Recode key variables (readable labels)


Show/Hide Code
df <- raw |>
  mutate(
    # categorical (labels inferred from frequency patterns and paper description)
    cpro = factor(cpro),

    cgen = factor(cgen, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("Male", "Female")),
    crac = factor(crac, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("Kinh", "Minorities")),
    cjob = factor(cjob, levels = c(1, 2, 3),
                  labels = c("Agriculture", "Service/Industry", "Other")),

    # keep quintile ordered for descriptive plots; not used as predictor in regression below
    cqui = factor(cqui, levels = 1:5, labels = paste("Quintile", 1:5), ordered = TRUE),

    # IMPORTANT: keep education as a normal factor for interpretable regression output
    fedu = factor(fedu, levels = 0:3,
                  labels = c("Primary", "Lower secondary", "Upper secondary", "Other"),
                  ordered = FALSE),

    fvtp = factor(fvtp, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("Short course", "Long course")),
    fcra = factor(fcra, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("Not accessible", "Accessible")),
    ftap = factor(ftap, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("Low", "Adequate")),

    # numeric
    tein = as.numeric(tein),
    tain = as.numeric(tain),
    tsii = as.numeric(tsii),
    toin = as.numeric(toin),

    log_tein = log1p(tein)
  )

title: Take-Home Exercise 01
text:
3. Key insights (visual EDA)

The following section provides 10 insights.
To keep visuals clean, each plot filters out missing values for the variables used (so you won’t see a literal “NA” group on axes).



Insight 1 — Total income is strongly right-skewed


Show/Hide Code
p1a <- df |>
  filter(!is.na(tein)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = tein)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 35, fill = '#4C78A8', alpha = 0.85, color = 'white') +
  income_x() +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Total Income (TEIN)",
    subtitle = "A long right tail implies income inequality within the sample",
    x = "Total income",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  col_dark2() +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')
p1b <- df |>
  filter(!is.na(log_tein)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = log_tein)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 35, fill = '#4C78A8', alpha = 0.85, color = 'white') +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution on log(1 + TEIN) scale",
    subtitle = "Log scale improves comparability when extreme values exist",
    x = "log(1 + total income)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  col_dark2() +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')
p1a / p1b









Interpretation. TEIN is right-skewed: most respondents cluster at lower income levels while a smaller group earns much more. This can inflate the mean relative to the median. For later comparisons, distribution-focused plots and log-scale views help avoid conclusions driven by a few extreme values.



Insight 2 — Income composition differs by source


Show/Hide Code
comp_long <- df |>
  select(tain, tsii, toin) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "source", values_to = "income") |>
  mutate(source = recode(source,
                         tain = "Agriculture",
                         tsii = "Service/Industry",
                         toin = "Other"))

ggplot(comp_long, aes(x = income)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  facet_wrap(~ source, scales = "free_x", ncol = 1) +
  income_x() +
  labs(
    title = "Income components: distributions differ by source",
    x = "Income component",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  col_dark2() +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')









Interpretation. Agriculture-related income appears central for many respondents, while service/industry income tends to show wider dispersion. This supports analysing career sector and income composition, not only total income.



Insight 3 — Education is associated with higher income, but overlap remains


Show/Hide Code
df |>
  filter(!is.na(tein), !is.na(fedu)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fedu, y = tein)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.6, width = 0.6, .width = 0.9) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.18, outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.55) +
  income_y() +
  income_y() +
  fill_set2() +
  labs(
    title = "Total income by education level",
    x = "Education (FEDU)",
    y = "Total income (TEIN)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")









Interpretation. Higher education levels tend to shift income upward, yet group overlap remains large. Education likely helps, but constraints such as job type, credit access, and local labour markets may limit whether education translates into higher earnings.



Insight 4 — Vocational training is linked to higher mean income (with uncertainty)


Show/Hide Code
train_sum <- df |>
  filter(!is.na(tein), !is.na(fvtp)) |>
  group_by(fvtp) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_tein = mean(tein),
    se = sd(tein) / sqrt(n),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(train_sum, aes(x = fvtp, y = mean_tein)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = '#F58518') +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_tein - 1.96 * se, ymax = mean_tein + 1.96 * se), width = 0.15) +
  income_y() +
  labs(
    title = "Mean income by vocational training (95% CI)",
    x = "Training type",
    y = "Mean TEIN"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  col_dark2() +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')









Interpretation. Longer vocational training shows higher mean income, and the confidence intervals provide uncertainty context. This supports (associationally) the idea that job-relevant skill accumulation may increase earnings.



Insight 5 — Credit accessibility shows an upward shift in income


Show/Hide Code
df |>
  filter(!is.na(tein), !is.na(fcra)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fcra, y = tein)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.7, width = 0.6, .width = 0.9) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.18, outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.55) +
  income_y() +
  income_y() +
  fill_set2() +
  labs(
    title = "Total income by credit accessibility",
    x = "Credit accessibility (FCRA)",
    y = "Total income (TEIN)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  col_dark2() +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')









Interpretation. Respondents with credit access show a modest upward shift, especially in the upper tail. Credit may enable investment in inputs/tools or diversification into non-farm activities, but overlap suggests it is not the only driver.



Insight 6 — Technological application is modestly associated with income


Show/Hide Code
df |>
  filter(!is.na(tein), !is.na(ftap)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = ftap, y = tein)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.7, width = 0.6, .width = 0.9) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.18, outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.55) +
  income_y() +
  income_y() +
  fill_set2() +
  labs(
    title = "Total income by technological application",
    x = "Technology application (FTAP)",
    y = "Total income (TEIN)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  col_dark2() +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')









Interpretation. Adequate technology adoption shows a small but consistent upward shift. The modest size implies technology may complement (not replace) broader constraints such as market access and job availability.



Insight 7 — Career sector is one of the strongest separators


Show/Hide Code
p7a <- df |>
  filter(!is.na(tein), !is.na(cjob)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = cjob, y = tein)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.6, width = 0.6, .width = 0.9) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.18, outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.55) +
  income_y() +
  income_y() +
  fill_set2() +
  labs(
    title = "Total income by career sector",
    x = "Career sector (CJOB)",
    y = "Total income (TEIN)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  col_dark2() +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')
p7b <- df |>
  filter(!is.na(log_tein), !is.na(cjob)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = cjob, y = log_tein)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Career sector comparison on log scale",
    x = "Career sector",
    y = "log(1 + TEIN)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  col_dark2() +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')
p7a / p7b









Interpretation. Income differences across career sectors remain clear even after log transformation, implying structural differences in earning opportunities. This motivates controlling for career sector in the multivariate model.



Insight 8 — Gender and race gaps exist, but are smaller than career effects


Show/Hide Code
p8a <- df |>
  filter(!is.na(tein), !is.na(cgen)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = cgen, y = tein)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.7) +
  income_y() +
  labs(title = "Total income by gender", x = "Gender", y = "TEIN") +
  theme_minimal() +
  col_dark2() +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')
p8b <- df |>
  filter(!is.na(tein), !is.na(crac)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = crac, y = tein)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.7) +
  income_y() +
  labs(title = "Total income by race", x = "Race", y = "TEIN") +
  theme_minimal() +
  col_dark2() +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')
p8a | p8b









Interpretation. Gender and race show income differences, but the separation is generally weaker than the career-sector split. These patterns are still important for equity discussions, but structural labour segmentation may dominate.



Insight 9 — Province-level variation exists (exploratory)


Show/Hide Code
df |>
  filter(!is.na(tein), !is.na(cpro)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = cpro, y = tein)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.75) +
  income_y() +
  labs(
    title = "Income differences across provinces (coded)",
    subtitle = "Exploratory view; mapping codes to province names would improve interpretation",
    x = "Province (CPRO code)",
    y = "TEIN"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  col_dark2() +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')









Interpretation. Income varies across provinces, potentially reflecting differences in infrastructure, market connectivity, and local job opportunities. Without explicit province-name mapping, this insight is treated as exploratory.



Insight 10 — Correlation structure among attitudinal/behaviour items
This highlight uses a lower-triangle correlation matrix with hierarchical clustering order to reveal groups of items that move together.
In practice, this helps decide whether items should be summarised into composite scores (instead of analysing each item independently).

10.1 Build a robust correlation dataset (avoid n = 0)


Show/Hide Code
# 1) Select candidate items (prefix-based)
candidate_items <- df |>
  select(matches("^(lho|ppo|aro)\\d+$"), any_of(c("lcre", "lsav", "lwda"))) |>
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric))

# 2) Keep columns with reasonable completeness (<= 50% missing) and variability
keep_cols <- candidate_items |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ mean(is.na(.)))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "var", values_to = "missing_rate") |>
  filter(missing_rate <= 0.50) |>
  pull(var)

corr_vars <- candidate_items |>
  select(all_of(keep_cols)) |>
  select(where(~ n_distinct(., na.rm = TRUE) > 1))

dim(corr_vars)


[1] 725  11




10.2 Correlation matrix (pairwise complete)


Show/Hide Code
ggstatsplot::ggcorrmat(
  data = corr_vars,
  cor.vars = 1:ncol(corr_vars),
  type = "parametric",
  matrix.type = "lower",
  hc.order = TRUE,
  pairwise = TRUE,
  title = "Correlation among perception/behaviour items",
  subtitle = "Pearson correlation (lower triangle); reordered by clustering"
)









Interpretation. Clusters of moderately positive correlations suggest that respondents rate some items consistently, hinting at broader latent dimensions (e.g., perceived constraints or behavioural tendencies). Near-zero correlations indicate relatively independent signals. This structure can guide follow-up modelling (e.g., creating composite indices or applying PCA) rather than treating each item as separate.

title: Take-Home Exercise 01
text:
4. Multivariate summary (interpretive)
To summarise patterns while accounting for multiple factors, I fit a simple linear model. The aim is interpretive comparison, not causal inference.

4.1 Model (clean modelling dataset)


Show/Hide Code
model_df <- df |>
  select(tein, fedu, fvtp, fcra, ftap, cjob, cgen, crac) |>
  drop_na()

# sanity check: make sure training has both levels in the model data
model_df |> count(fvtp)


# A tibble: 2 × 2
  fvtp             n
  <fct>        <int>
1 Short course   504
2 Long course     65




Show/Hide Code
m1 <- lm(tein ~ fedu + fcra + ftap + cjob + cgen + crac, data = model_df)
broom::tidy(m1, conf.int = TRUE)


# A tibble: 10 × 7
   term                 estimate std.error statistic  p.value conf.low conf.high
   <chr>                   <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>

 2 feduLower secondary    15.2        2.23     6.83  2.17e-11   10.8       19.6 
 3 feduUpper secondary    24.3        3.16     7.69  6.64e-14   18.1       30.5 
 4 feduOther              75.5        5.21    14.5   1.34e-40   65.3       85.8 
 5 fcraAccessible         -0.398      2.51    -0.159 8.74e- 1   -5.33       4.53
 6 ftapAdequate            2.59       1.71     1.51  1.32e- 1   -0.783      5.95
 7 cjobService/Industry    6.18       3.54     1.74  8.15e- 2   -0.776     13.1 
 8 cjobOther               5.83       2.68     2.18  2.98e- 2    0.574     11.1

10 cracMinorities          1.47       1.78     0.825 4.10e- 1   -2.03       4.96




4.2 Coefficient plot (95% CI)


Show/Hide Code
coef_tbl <- broom::tidy(m1, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)")

ggplot(coef_tbl, aes(x = estimate, y = reorder(term, estimate), color = estimate > 0)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(size = 2.7) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = conf.low, xmax = conf.high), height = 0.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Income determinants (linear model on log(1 + TEIN))",
    subtitle = "Coefficients with 95% confidence intervals (interpretive)",
    x = "Estimated association with log(1 + TEIN)",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  col_dark2() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")









Interpretation. After controlling for demographics and career sector, the EDA patterns often remain directionally consistent, but the magnitude differs. Career sector typically shows one of the strongest associations, reinforcing the role of structural segmentation. Education, training, credit access and technology tend to contribute in smaller but meaningful ways.

title: Take-Home Exercise 01
text:
5. Conclusion
Main takeaways 1. Total income is strongly right-skewed, implying inequality within the sample. 2. Agriculture is a key income source for many respondents; non-agricultural income is more dispersed. 3. Education and vocational training are associated with higher income, but overlap indicates constraints remain. 4. Credit access and technology application show modest, directionally positive relationships with income. 5. Career sector is a major separator of earnings. 6. Correlation clustering suggests some attitudinal/behaviour items represent broader underlying dimensions.
Limitations - Sampling and survey design limit causal claims and generalisability. - Province codes would benefit from explicit name mapping for stronger geographic interpretation.
Next steps - Construct composite indices from clustered items (or apply PCA) and test whether they explain income beyond demographics. - Consider robust modelling (e.g., log-income or quantile regression) given skewness.

title: Hands-on Exercise 09: Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data
text:
In this hands-on exercise, I worked on a communication network dataset and used R to model, visualise and analyse network structure. Compared with ordinary tabular analysis, network analysis focuses more on relationships among actors. In this case, employees are represented as nodes and email interactions are represented as edges.
This chapter is useful because it shows how tidy data can be transformed into graph objects and then explored from different angles, such as layout, centrality, community structure and interactive visualisation.

title: Hands-on Exercise 09: Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data

In this hands-on exercise, I worked on a communication network dataset and used R to model, visualise and analyse network structure. Compared with ordinary tabular analysis, network analysis focuses more on relationships among actors. In this case, employees are represented as nodes and email interactions are represented as edges.
This chapter is useful because it shows how tidy data can be transformed into graph objects and then explored from different angles, such as layout, centrality, community structure and interactive visualisation.

3.4 27.3.4 Helper functions for robust wrangling


Code
pick_first_existing <- function(df, candidates) {
  nm <- intersect(candidates, names(df))
  if (length(nm) == 0) NA_character_ else nm[1]
}

parse_sent_datetime <- function(x) {
  suppressWarnings(
    lubridate::parse_date_time(
      x,
      orders = c(
        "mdy HMS", "mdy HM", "mdy IMS p", "mdy IM p", "mdy",
        "ymd HMS", "ymd HM", "ymd",
        "dmy HMS", "dmy HM", "dmy"
      )
    )
  )
}




3.5 27.3.5 Wrangling the data


Code
edge_from_col <- pick_first_existing(edges_raw, c("source", "from", "sender", "src"))
edge_to_col   <- pick_first_existing(edges_raw, c("target", "to", "receiver", "dst"))
edge_date_col <- pick_first_existing(edges_raw, c("sentdate", "sent_date", "date", "timestamp", "time"))

node_id_col    <- pick_first_existing(nodes_raw, c("id", "name", "label", "email"))
node_label_col <- pick_first_existing(nodes_raw, c("label", "name", "id", "email"))
node_dept_col  <- pick_first_existing(nodes_raw, c("department", "dept", "unit", "division"))
node_title_col <- pick_first_existing(nodes_raw, c("title", "position", "role", "job_title"))

edges_clean <- edges_raw %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), as.character)) %>%
  transmute(
    from = .data[[edge_from_col]],
    to   = .data[[edge_to_col]],
    sent_dt = if (!is.na(edge_date_col)) parse_sent_datetime(.data[[edge_date_col]]) else as.POSIXct(NA),
    sent_date = as.Date(sent_dt)
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(from), !is.na(to), from != to) %>%
  mutate(
    weekday = weekdays(sent_date),
    weekday = factor(
      weekday,
      levels = c("Sunday", "Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday", "Thursday", "Friday", "Saturday"),
      ordered = TRUE
    )
  )

nodes_clean <- nodes_raw %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), as.character)) %>%
  transmute(
    name = .data[[node_id_col]],
    label = .data[[node_label_col]],
    department = if (!is.na(node_dept_col)) .data[[node_dept_col]] else "Unknown",
    title = if (!is.na(node_title_col)) .data[[node_title_col]] else "Unknown"
  ) %>%
  distinct(name, .keep_all = TRUE)

missing_nodes <- tibble(
  name = setdiff(unique(c(edges_clean$from, edges_clean$to)), nodes_clean$name),
  label = setdiff(unique(c(edges_clean$from, edges_clean$to)), nodes_clean$name),
  department = "Unknown",
  title = "Unknown"
)

nodes_clean <- bind_rows(nodes_clean, missing_nodes) %>%
  distinct(name, .keep_all = TRUE)

title: Hands-on Exercise 09: Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data
text:
4 27.4 Creating Network Objects Using tidygraph

4.1 27.4.1 The tbl_graph object


Code
graph_tbl <- igraph::graph_from_data_frame(
  d = edges_total,
  vertices = nodes_clean,
  directed = TRUE
) %>%
  tidygraph::as_tbl_graph()




4.2 27.4.2 Adding node metrics


Code
graph_metrics <- graph_tbl %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  mutate(
    indegree = centrality_degree(mode = "in"),
    outdegree = centrality_degree(mode = "out"),
    degree = centrality_degree(mode = "all"),
    betweenness = centrality_betweenness(directed = TRUE),
    closeness = centrality_closeness(mode = "out"),
    pagerank = centrality_pagerank(directed = TRUE)
  )




4.3 27.4.3 Preparing subgraphs for clearer plots


Code
set.seed(608)

top_nodes <- graph_metrics %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  slice_max(order_by = degree, n = 35) %>%
  pull(name)

graph_top <- graph_metrics %>%
  convert(to_subgraph, name %in% top_nodes)

graph_undirected <- graph_metrics %>%
  to_undirected() %>%
  convert(to_simple)

title: Hands-on Exercise 09: Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data
section: 5 27.5 Plotting Static Network Graphs with ggraph
text:
5 27.5 Plotting Static Network Graphs with ggraph

5.1 27.5.1 Plotting a basic network graph


Code
set.seed(608)

ggraph(graph_top, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.25, colour = "grey45") +
  geom_node_point(size = 2.3, colour = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Basic network graph",
    subtitle = "Top employees by overall degree"
  )









Observation: A dense core appears in the middle of the graph, while several smaller nodes stay around the edges.
Insight: This suggests that communication is concentrated around a relatively small group of employees rather than being evenly distributed across the organisation.


5.2 27.5.2 Changing the default network graph theme


Code
set.seed(608)

ggraph(graph_top, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.25, colour = "grey50") +
  geom_node_point(size = 2.5, colour = "black") +
  theme_graph() +
  labs(
    title = "Basic network graph with theme_graph()",
    subtitle = "A cleaner graph theme improves readability"
  )









Observation: The graph looks cleaner after removing the usual axes and panel elements.
Insight: For network plots, axes do not usually carry direct meaning, so a graph-specific theme helps the audience focus on structure instead.


5.3 27.5.3 Changing the coloring of the plot


Code
set.seed(608)

ggraph(graph_top, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.4, colour = "#FA8072") +
  geom_node_point(size = 2.8, colour = "#FDE725") +
  theme_graph(background = "grey10") +
  labs(
    title = "Network graph with customised colour scheme",
    subtitle = "High contrast improves visual emphasis"
  )









Observation: The dark background makes the nodes and links stand out much more clearly.
Insight: Colour is not just decorative. In dense network visualisation, a good contrast can improve readability a lot.


5.4 27.5.4 Working with graph layouts


Code
layout_tbl <- tidyr::crossing(
  layout_name = c("fr", "kk", "graphopt", "stress", "circle", "dh"),
  graph_id = 1
)

set.seed(608)

layout_plots <- purrr::map(layout_tbl$layout_name, function(lay) {
  ggraph(graph_top, layout = lay) +
    geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.25, colour = "grey55") +
    geom_node_point(aes(size = degree), colour = "#F46D43") +
    scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 7), guide = "none") +
    theme_graph() +
    labs(title = paste("layout =", lay))
})

patchwork::wrap_plots(layout_plots, ncol = 3)









Observation: Different layouts produce very different visual impressions even though the underlying network is exactly the same.
Insight: Layout selection matters a lot in network analysis. A poor layout can hide structure, while a suitable layout can reveal clusters, bridges and hubs more clearly.


5.5 27.5.5 Fruchterman-Reingold layout with centrality-based node size


Code
set.seed(608)

ggraph(graph_metrics, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.10, colour = "grey65") +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree), colour = "#F46D43", alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1.5, 10)) +
  theme_graph() +
  labs(
    title = "Fruchterman-Reingold layout",
    subtitle = "Node size is mapped to degree centrality"
  )









Observation: A few nodes are much larger than the rest, which means they have many more connections.
Insight: Degree centrality is a direct and intuitive way to identify communication hubs in this network.


5.6 27.5.6 Adding and removing vertices and edges to form the network


Code
set.seed(608)

graph_filtered <- graph_metrics %>%
  activate(edges) %>%
  filter(weight >= quantile(weight, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  filter(degree >= quantile(degree, 0.60, na.rm = TRUE))

ggraph(graph_filtered, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.35, colour = "grey35") +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, colour = department)) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(3, 10)) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  theme_graph() +
  labs(
    title = "Filtered network",
    subtitle = "Only stronger edges and more connected employees are retained",
    colour = "Department"
  )









Observation: After filtering, the overall structure becomes much less cluttered and the main communication backbone is easier to see.
Insight: Removing weaker edges is often useful in practice because it helps distinguish stable communication patterns from occasional contact.


5.7 27.5.7 Modifying edge aesthetics


Code
set.seed(608)

ggraph(graph_filtered, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width = weight, alpha = weight), colour = "grey20") +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, colour = department)) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.3, 2.2)) +
  scale_edge_alpha(range = c(0.15, 0.75)) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(3, 10)) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_graph() +
  labs(
    title = "Network with edge aesthetics mapped to communication intensity",
    subtitle = "Thicker and darker lines represent stronger email ties",
    colour = "Department"
  )









Observation: Some links become much more prominent after width and alpha are mapped to edge weight.
Insight: Edge aesthetics are helpful because not all connections are equally important. Weight can reveal which ties are more active or persistent.

title: Hands-on Exercise 09: Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data
section: 6 27.6 Creating Forest Graphs and Faceted Graphs
text:
6 27.6 Creating Forest Graphs and Faceted Graphs

6.1 27.6.1 Creating forest graphs
To create a more tree-like view, I select the strongest outgoing ties from a highly connected employee and visualise a local ego network.


Code
central_node <- graph_metrics %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  slice_max(order_by = outdegree, n = 1) %>%
  pull(name)

ego_vertices <- igraph::ego(as.igraph(graph_metrics), order = 1, nodes = central_node, mode = "all")[[1]]
forest_graph <- induced_subgraph(as.igraph(graph_metrics), vids = ego_vertices) %>%
  as_tbl_graph() %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  mutate(local_degree = centrality_degree())

set.seed(608)

ggraph(forest_graph, layout = "tree") +
  geom_edge_link(colour = "grey45") +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = local_degree, colour = department)) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = label), repel = TRUE, size = 3) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  theme_graph() +
  labs(
    title = "Tree-like local ego network",
    subtitle = paste("A local communication structure around", central_node),
    colour = "Department"
  )









Observation: The tree layout highlights who is directly connected to the central employee and whether those contacts are spread across different departments.
Insight: This kind of local view is helpful when the full network is too dense and I want to focus on one person or one communication neighbourhood.


6.2 27.6.2 Working with facet_edges()


Code
edge_weekday_tbl <- edges_agg %>%
  filter(!is.na(weekday)) %>%
  count(weekday, sort = TRUE)

top_weekdays <- edge_weekday_tbl %>%
  slice_head(n = 4) %>%
  pull(weekday) %>%
  as.character()

graph_by_weekday <- igraph::graph_from_data_frame(
  edges_agg %>% filter(as.character(weekday) %in% top_weekdays),
  vertices = nodes_clean,
  directed = TRUE
) %>%
  as_tbl_graph()

set.seed(608)

ggraph(graph_by_weekday, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(alpha = weight), colour = "grey35") +
  geom_node_point(colour = "#F46D43", size = 2.8) +
  facet_edges(~weekday) +
  theme_graph() +
  labs(
    title = "Faceted network by weekday using facet_edges()",
    subtitle = "Edges are separated by weekday while node positions remain stable"
  )









Observation: The number and density of edges vary across weekdays.
Insight: This suggests that communication intensity is not constant during the week. Some days appear busier and more interconnected than others.


6.3 27.6.3 A framed facet graph


Code
set.seed(608)

ggraph(graph_by_weekday, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(alpha = weight), colour = "#2C7FB8") +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = department), size = 2.5) +
  facet_edges(~weekday) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_graph() +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")) +
  labs(
    title = "Framed faceted network graph",
    subtitle = "Department colours help compare node groups across weekday panels",
    colour = "Department"
  )









Observation: The coloured nodes make it easier to compare whether the same departments remain active across different weekday panels.
Insight: Faceted graphs are useful because they keep the overall structure comparable while allowing one variable to change panel by panel.


6.4 27.6.4 Working with facet_nodes()


Code
major_dept <- nodes_clean %>%
  count(department, sort = TRUE) %>%
  slice_head(n = 6) %>%
  pull(department)

graph_major_dept <- graph_metrics %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  mutate(department = if_else(department %in% major_dept, department, "Other"))

set.seed(608)

ggraph(graph_major_dept, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.08, colour = "grey55") +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = department, size = degree)) +
  facet_nodes(~department) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1.5, 8), guide = "none") +
  theme_graph() +
  labs(
    title = "Faceted network by department using facet_nodes()",
    subtitle = "Each panel highlights one department while preserving the common layout",
    colour = "Department"
  )









Observation: Some departments appear more central and densely connected than others.
Insight: This kind of view is useful for understanding whether communication is balanced across organisational units or dominated by a few key groups.

title: Hands-on Exercise 09: Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data
text:
7 27.7 Network Metrics Analysis

7.1 27.7.1 Computing centrality indices


Code
centrality_long <- graph_metrics %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  select(label, degree, indegree, outdegree, betweenness, closeness, pagerank) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = degree, n = 10) %>%
  pivot_longer(-label, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value")

ggplot(centrality_long, aes(x = reorder(label, value), y = value, fill = metric)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title = "Top 10 employees by degree and other centrality measures",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Metric value",
    fill = "Metric"
  )









Observation: The top employees under degree centrality also tend to score relatively high in some other metrics, but not always in exactly the same order.
Insight: Different centrality measures capture different meanings. Degree shows how connected a node is, while betweenness and closeness reflect more structural roles.


7.2 27.7.2 Visualising centrality relationships


Code
node_metrics_tbl <- graph_metrics %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  as_tibble()

ggplot(node_metrics_tbl, aes(x = degree, y = betweenness, colour = department)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.75, size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.6, colour = "grey25") +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship between degree and betweenness",
    subtitle = "More connected employees often also play bridging roles",
    x = "Degree centrality",
    y = "Betweenness centrality",
    colour = "Department"
  )









Observation: Nodes with higher degree often have higher betweenness, although there is still noticeable variation.
Insight: This means some highly connected employees are also important bridges, but a node can be popular without necessarily controlling many shortest paths.


7.3 27.7.3 Visualising network matrix


Code
mat_nodes <- graph_metrics %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  slice_max(order_by = degree, n = 20) %>%
  pull(name)

mat_graph <- graph_metrics %>%
  convert(to_subgraph, name %in% mat_nodes)

adj_mat <- as.matrix(igraph::as_adjacency_matrix(as.igraph(mat_graph), attr = "weight", sparse = FALSE))

adj_df <- as.data.frame(adj_mat) %>%
  mutate(from = rownames(adj_mat)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-from, names_to = "to", values_to = "weight")

ggplot(adj_df, aes(x = from, y = to, fill = log1p(weight))) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "C") +
  coord_equal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Adjacency matrix view of the top 20 employees",
    x = "Sender",
    y = "Receiver",
    fill = "log1p(weight)"
  )









Observation: The matrix shows clusters of stronger communication, as well as several sender-receiver pairs with almost no activity.
Insight: A matrix view complements node-link diagrams well because it reduces overlap and helps detect dense blocks more clearly.


7.4 27.7.4 Visualising community


Code
community_obj <- cluster_louvain(as.igraph(graph_undirected), weights = E(as.igraph(graph_undirected))$weight)

graph_community <- graph_undirected %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  mutate(community = as.factor(membership(community_obj)))

set.seed(608)

ggraph(graph_community, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.12, colour = "grey60") +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = community, size = degree)) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 9)) +
  theme_graph() +
  labs(
    title = "Community structure of the communication network",
    subtitle = "Louvain clustering reveals subgroups inside the organisation",
    colour = "Community"
  )









Observation: The network can be separated into several communities instead of forming one completely homogeneous mass.
Insight: Community detection is useful because it helps reveal substructures that may correspond to departments, work teams, or recurring collaboration groups.

title: Hands-on Exercise 09: Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data
section: 8 27.8 Building Interactive Network Graph with visNetwork
text:
8 27.8 Building Interactive Network Graph with visNetwork

8.1 27.8.1 Data preparation


Code
vis_nodes <- graph_community %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(
    id = name,
    label = label,
    value = scales::rescale(degree, to = c(8, 35)),
    group = department,
    title = paste0(
      "<p><b>", label, "</b><br>",
      "Department: ", department, "<br>",
      "Title: ", title, "<br>",
      "Degree: ", round(degree, 0), "<br>",
      "Betweenness: ", round(betweenness, 2), "<br>",
      "Community: ", community, "</p>"
    )
  ) %>%
  select(id, label, value, group, title, community)

edge_tbl <- graph_community %>%
  activate(edges) %>%
  as_tibble()

if (!"weight" %in% names(edge_tbl)) {
  edge_tbl <- edge_tbl %>%
    mutate(weight = 1)
}

vis_edges <- edge_tbl %>%
  transmute(
    from,
    to,
    value = weight,
    title = paste0("Email count: ", weight),
    arrows = "to"
  )




8.2 27.8.2 Plotting the first interactive network graph


Code
visNetwork(vis_nodes, vis_edges, width = "100%", height = "650px") %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = "layout_with_fr") %>%
  visEdges(smooth = FALSE, color = list(opacity = 0.35)) %>%
  visNodes(borderWidth = 1) %>%
  visOptions(highlightNearest = TRUE, nodesIdSelection = TRUE) %>%
  visLegend()






Observation: The interactive graph is much easier to explore because I can zoom, drag, hover and inspect node details.
Insight: For dense networks, interactivity is very useful because static plots can quickly become crowded when many nodes and edges are shown together.


8.3 27.8.3 Working with layout


Code
visNetwork(vis_nodes, vis_edges, width = "100%", height = "650px") %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = "layout_nicely") %>%
  visEdges(smooth = FALSE, color = list(opacity = 0.30)) %>%
  visPhysics(solver = "forceAtlas2Based") %>%
  visOptions(highlightNearest = list(enabled = TRUE, degree = 1)) %>%
  visInteraction(navigationButtons = TRUE) %>%
  visLegend()






Observation: Different interactive layouts arrange the same network in slightly different ways.
Insight: Just like static layouts, interactive layouts also affect how easily users can interpret clusters and hubs.


8.4 27.8.4 Working with interactive buttons: groups


Code
visNetwork(vis_nodes, vis_edges, width = "100%", height = "650px") %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = "layout_with_kk") %>%
  visEdges(smooth = FALSE, color = list(opacity = 0.25)) %>%
  visOptions(
    highlightNearest = TRUE,
    selectedBy = "group",
    nodesIdSelection = TRUE
  ) %>%
  visInteraction(navigationButtons = TRUE) %>%
  visLegend()






Observation: Group-based filtering helps focus on one department at a time and reduces visual overload.
Insight: This function is practical for presentations because users can quickly check where one organisational group sits inside the wider network.


8.5 27.8.5 Working with interactive buttons: community


Code
vis_nodes_comm <- vis_nodes %>%
  mutate(group = paste("Community", community))

visNetwork(vis_nodes_comm, vis_edges, width = "100%", height = "650px") %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = "layout_with_fr") %>%
  visEdges(smooth = FALSE, color = list(opacity = 0.25)) %>%
  visOptions(
    highlightNearest = TRUE,
    selectedBy = "group",
    nodesIdSelection = TRUE
  ) %>%
  visInteraction(navigationButtons = TRUE) %>%
  visLegend()






Observation: When the nodes are grouped by community instead of department, the subgroup structure becomes even more obvious.
Insight: This shows that detected communication communities are not necessarily identical to formal organisational departments.

title: Hands-on Exercise 09: Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data
text:
9 27.9 Conclusion
Overall, this exercise helped me understand that network analysis is not only about drawing connected dots. It also involves choosing suitable graph objects, layouts, aesthetics and metrics to reveal meaningful structure. In this email network, I observed a dense core, several important hub nodes, and a visible community structure.
From a student perspective, the most useful lesson is that one network can look very different depending on how it is visualised. Because of that, it is important to compare more than one layout or metric before making conclusions.

title: Hands-on Exercise 09: Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data
text:
10 27.10 References
Wickham, H., Navarro, D., & Pedersen, T. L. (2024). ggplot2: Elegant Graphics for Data Analysis.
Pedersen, T. L. (2024). ggraph: An Implementation of Grammar of Graphics for Graphs and Networks.
Csardi, G., & Nepusz, T. (2006). The igraph software package for complex network research.
Almende B.V. and Contributors. (2024). visNetwork: Network Visualization using vis.js Library.

title: Hands-on Exercise 8B: Visualising Geospatial Point Data
text:
4 22.4 Drawing Proportional Symbol Map

4.1 22.4.1 Start with interactive mode


Code
tmap_mode("view")




4.2 22.4.2 Basic interactive point symbol map


Code
tm_shape(sgpools_sf) +
  tm_bubbles(
    fill = "red",
    size = 1,
    col = "black",
    lwd = 1
  )






Student note: This version is useful for checking the outlet locations quickly, but all points still look equally important because the size is fixed.


4.3 22.4.3 Make it proportional


Code
tm_shape(sgpools_sf) +
  tm_bubbles(
    fill = "red",
    size = "Gp1Gp2 Winnings",
    col = "black",
    lwd = 1
  )







Observation: Once symbol size is linked to winnings, the large-value outlets become much easier to spot. This is a more meaningful design because size is now encoding data rather than acting as decoration.


4.4 22.4.4 Add outlet type as colour


Code
tm_shape(sgpools_sf) +
  tm_bubbles(
    fill = "OUTLET TYPE",
    size = "Gp1Gp2 Winnings",
    col = "black",
    lwd = 1
  )








Insight: Combining size and colour adds another layer of comparison. I can now compare both outlet type and winnings at the same time, although too many visual variables would eventually make the map harder to read.


4.5 22.4.5 Faceted interactive map


Code
tm_shape(sgpools_sf) +
  tm_bubbles(
    fill = "OUTLET TYPE",
    size = "Gp1Gp2 Winnings",
    col = "black",
    lwd = 1
  ) +
  tm_facets(
    by = "OUTLET TYPE",
    nrow = 1,
    sync = TRUE
  )

















Student note: The synced faceted view is helpful because it separates Branch and Outlet into two windows while keeping the same zoom level. That makes the comparison much cleaner.


4.6 22.4.6 Switch back to plot mode


Code
tmap_mode("plot")

title: Hands-on Exercise 8B: Visualising Geospatial Point Data
text:
5 22.5 Short reflection
Compared with choropleth maps, proportional symbol maps are better when the data is attached to exact locations rather than areas. In this example, symbol size is a natural choice because winnings are numeric and vary by outlet. The faceted interactive map is my favourite version because it balances detail and readability.

title: Hands-on Exercise 07: Time-oriented Data Visual Analytics
text:
This hands-on focuses on time-oriented visual analytics. I follow the teacher’s workflow but add short student-style notes (what I observed, what I think it implies), while keeping the page clean and Netlify-friendly.
We will build:

Calendar heatmap (weekday × hour) for attack rhythms,
Cycle plot (month panels across years) for seasonality,
Slopegraph-style comparison (two time points) for changes.

We will build:

Calendar heatmap (weekday × hour) for attack rhythms,

title: Hands-on Exercise 07: Time-oriented Data Visual Analytics
text:
2 17.2 Setup

2.1 17.2.1 Packages


Code
pacman::p_load(
  tidyverse, lubridate, readxl, knitr,
  viridis, ggthemes, ggrepel
)




2.2 17.2.2 Locale fix (important on Chinese Windows)
On Chinese systems, weekdays() can return weekday names in Chinese (e.g., 星期一).
That will not match English factor levels and can turn wkday into NA.


Code
Sys.setlocale("LC_TIME", "English")


[1] "English_United States.1252"



If "English" fails on your machine, try "C" or "en_US.UTF-8".

3.2 17.4.2 Data preparation (robust timezone handling)
In practice, the tz column may contain invalid or missing timezone strings.
lubridate::with_tz() requires a valid Olson/IANA timezone name, otherwise rendering fails.
Strategy: - force tz to character, - replace invalid / empty tz with "UTC", - parse timestamps as UTC first, then convert to local tz, - extract weekday and hour.


Code
make_hr_wkday <- function(ts, tz) {

  tz <- as.character(tz)[1]

  tz <- dplyr::if_else(
    is.na(tz) | tz == "" | !(tz %in% OlsonNames()),
    "UTC",
    tz
  )

  real_times <- with_tz(ymd_hms(ts, tz = "UTC"), tz = tz)

  tibble(
    wkday = weekdays(real_times),
    hour  = hour(real_times)
  )
}

wkday_levels <- c("Sunday", "Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday",
                  "Thursday", "Friday", "Saturday")

attacks <- attacks_raw %>%
  mutate(
    tz = as.character(tz),
    tz = dplyr::if_else(is.na(tz) | tz == "" | !(tz %in% OlsonNames()), "UTC", tz)
  ) %>%
  group_by(tz) %>%
  group_modify(~ bind_cols(.x, make_hr_wkday(.x$timestamp, unique(.x$tz)[1]))) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    wkday = factor(wkday, levels = wkday_levels),
    hour  = factor(hour, levels = 0:23)
  )

5.2 17.6.2 Prepare dataset (top countries, 1961 vs 1980)


Code
rice2 <- rice %>%
  filter(Year %in% c(1961, 1980)) %>%
  mutate(Year = factor(Year, levels = c(1961, 1980)))

top_countries <- rice2 %>%
  group_by(Country) %>%
  summarise(avg_yield = mean(Yield, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_yield)) %>%
  slice(1:12) %>%
  pull(Country)

  filter(Country %in% top_countries) %>%
  select(Country, Year, Yield) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Year, values_from = Yield) %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  mutate(Country = fct_reorder(Country, `1980`))



PlotCode




Code
p_slope <- ggplot(rice_wide) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 1, xend = 2, y = `1961`, yend = `1980`),
               linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.85, color = "gray35") +
  geom_point(aes(x = 1, y = `1961`), size = 2.2) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 2, y = `1980`), size = 2.2) +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(
    aes(x = 1, y = `1961`, label = Country),
    direction = "y", hjust = 1.1, size = 3,
    segment.color = "gray70", max.overlaps = Inf
  ) +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(
    aes(x = 2, y = `1980`, label = Country),
    direction = "y", hjust = -0.1, size = 3,
    segment.color = "gray70", max.overlaps = Inf
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1, 2), labels = c("1961", "1980")) +
  labs(
    title = "Rice yield change of top Asian countries: 1961 vs 1980",
    subtitle = "Slope indicates direction and magnitude of change",
    x = NULL, y = "Yield"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Helvetica") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(5.5, 50, 5.5, 50))

p_slope













Code
# ggrepel prevents label overlap without relying on fragile slopegraph packages





Observation: Countries with steeper upward slopes improved yield substantially from 1961 to 1980.
Insight: This two-point comparison is easy to read, but it hides intermediate-year volatility. A small-multiples time series would complement it well.

title: Hands-on Exercise 07: Time-oriented Data Visual Analytics
text:
6 17.8 Conclusion

Locale (LC_TIME) is critical on non-English systems; otherwise weekday factors may become NA.
Clean timezone values before with_tz() to prevent rendering failures.
Calendar heatmap + cycle plot + slopegraph provide complementary views of time patterns: rhythm, seasonality, and change.

title: Hands-on Exercise 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
Parallel coordinates plots (PCP) are designed for high-dimensional numeric data.
They are powerful for interactive exploration (detecting clusters and patterns) but can be cluttered with many observations.
In this hands-on, we will:

create static PCP using GGally::ggparcoord(),
create interactive PCP using parallelPlot,
improve readability with scaling, grouping, facets and rotated axis labels.

title: Hands-on Exercise 13: Creating Ternary Plot with R
text:
Ternary plots are useful for three-part compositional data (e.g., Young / Active / Old population shares) where each observation is expressed as proportions that sum to 1 (or 100%).
In this hands-on, we will:

load required packages,
wrangle the Singapore residents dataset into three measures (Young, Active, Old),
create a static ternary plot using ggtern,
create an interactive ternary plot using plotly.

Ternary plots are useful for three-part compositional data (e.g., Young / Active / Old population shares) where each observation is expressed as proportions that sum to 1 (or 100%).
In this hands-on, we will:

text:
2 13.2 Installing and launching R packages

pacman::p_load(plotly, ggtern, tidyverse)

title: Hands-on Exercise 13: Creating Ternary Plot with R
text:
3 13.3 Data Preparation

3.1 13.3.1 Importing data

The dataset file is expected to be in data/ folder.
If your file path is different, update it accordingly.


pop_data <- read_csv("../data/respopagsex2000to2018_tidy.csv")



3.2 13.3.2 Preparing the data
We reshape the age group column into multiple columns (wide format) and derive:

YOUNG: sum of age groups (0–19),
ACTIVE: sum of age groups (20–64),
OLD: sum of age groups (65+),
TOTAL: overall total across all age groups.

Then we keep Year = 2018 (you can switch to other years).

agpop_mutated <- pop_data %>%
  mutate(Year = as.character(Year)) %>%
  spread(AG, Population) %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG  = rowSums(.[4:8],  na.rm = TRUE),
    ACTIVE = rowSums(.[9:16], na.rm = TRUE),
    OLD    = rowSums(.[17:21], na.rm = TRUE),
    TOTAL  = YOUNG + ACTIVE + OLD
  ) %>%
  filter(Year == "2018") %>%
  filter(TOTAL > 0)

title: Hands-on Exercise 13: Creating Ternary Plot with R
text:
4 13.4 Plotting Ternary Diagram with R

4.1 13.4.1 Plotting a static ternary diagram
Start with a minimal ternary scatter plot.

ggtern(data = agpop_mutated, aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +
  geom_point()








Add title and a cleaner theme.

ggtern(data = agpop_mutated, aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Population structure, 2018") +
  theme_rgbw()










4.2 13.4.2 Plotting an interactive ternary diagram (plotly)
We define small helper functions for annotation and axis formatting, then build a scatterternary chart.

# reusable function for creating annotation object
label <- function(txt) {
  list(
    text = txt,
    x = 0.1, y = 1,
    ax = 0, ay = 0,
    xref = "paper", yref = "paper",
    align = "center",
    font = list(family = "serif", size = 15, color = "white"),
    bgcolor = "#b3b3b3",
    bordercolor = "black",
    borderwidth = 2
  )
}

# reusable function for axis formatting
axis <- function(txt) {
  list(
    title = txt,
    tickformat = ".0%",
    tickfont = list(size = 10)
  )
}

ternaryAxes <- list(
  aaxis = axis("Young"),
  baxis = axis("Active"),
  caxis = axis("Old")
)

plot_ly(
  agpop_mutated,
  a = ~YOUNG,
  b = ~ACTIVE,
  c = ~OLD,
  type = "scatterternary",
  mode = "markers"
) %>%
  layout(
    annotations = label("Ternary Markers"),
    ternary = ternaryAxes
  )

title: Hands-on Exercise 13: Creating Ternary Plot with R
text:
5 Notes / Tips

If your points look “squeezed”, try converting counts to proportions first, e.g. YOUNG/TOTAL, etc.
You can encode categories (e.g., Planning Area) using color = ~PA in plot_ly() or aesthetic mapping in ggtern().

title: Hands-on Exercise 4D: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
In this hands-on exercise, I practise uncertainty visualisation using:

ggplot2 error bars (standard error + confidence intervals),
interactive error bars (plotly + crosstalk),
ggdist intervals, and
Hypothetical Outcome Plots (HOPs) with ungeviz.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4D: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
11.5.1 Standard error bars

ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      x = RACE,
      ymin = mean - se,
      ymax = mean + se
    ),
    width = 0.2,
    colour = "black",
    alpha = 0.9,
    linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = RACE, y = mean),
    stat = "identity",
    color = "red",
    size = 1.5,
    alpha = 1
  ) +
  ggtitle("Standard error of mean maths score by race")

title: Hands-on Exercise 4D: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
11.5.2 95% confidence interval error bars

ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      x = reorder(RACE, -mean),
      ymin = mean - 1.96 * se,
      ymax = mean + 1.96 * se
    ),
    width = 0.2,
    colour = "black",
    alpha = 0.9,
    linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = RACE, y = mean),
    stat = "identity",
    color = "red",
    size = 1.5,
    alpha = 1
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Maths score",
    title = "95% confidence interval of mean maths score by race"
  )

title: Hands-on Exercise 4D: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
11.5.3 Interactive error bars (99% CI)

shared_df <- SharedData$new(my_sum)

p_int <- ggplot(shared_df) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      x = reorder(RACE, -mean),

    colour = "black",
    alpha = 0.9,
    size = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = RACE, y = mean),

    color = "red",
    size = 1.5,
    alpha = 1
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Race",
    y = "Mean maths score",
    title = "Interactive error bars (99% CI)"

crosstalk::bscols(
  widths = c(4, 8),
  ggplotly(p_int, tooltip = c("x", "y")),

)

In this hands-on exercise, I learn how to create animated statistical graphics.
I try two methods:

gganimate
plotly

pacman::p_load(readxl, gifski, gapminder,
               plotly, gganimate, tidyverse)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3B
text:
4.2.2 Importing the data
In this exercise, I use the Data worksheet from GlobalPopulation.xls.

col <- c("Country", "Continent")

globalPop <- read_xls("../data/GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet = "Data") %>%
  mutate(across(col, as.factor)) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))

title: Hands-on Exercise 3B
section: 4.3.1 Building a static population bubble plot
text:
4.3.1 Building a static population bubble plot

ggplot(globalPop,
       aes(x = Old, y = Young,
           size = Population,
           colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7,
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Year: {frame_time}",
       x = "% Aged",
       y = "% Young")

title: Hands-on Exercise 3B
text:
4.3.2 Building the animated bubble plot
I add transition_time(Year) and ease_aes('linear').

p_anim <- ggplot(globalPop,
                 aes(x = Old, y = Young,
                     size = Population,
                     colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7,
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Year: {frame_time}",
       x = "% Aged",
       y = "% Young") +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear")

p_anim








(Optional) If I want to render as GIF:

animate(p_anim, renderer = gifski_renderer())

title: Hands-on Exercise 3B
section: 4.4.1 Building an animated bubble plot: ggplotly() method
text:
4.4.1 Building an animated bubble plot: ggplotly() method

gg <- ggplot(globalPop,
             aes(x = Old,
                 y = Young,
                 size = Population,
                 colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = Population,
                 frame = Year),
             alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = "% Aged",
       y = "% Young") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

ggplotly(gg)

section: 4.4.2 Building an animated bubble plot: plot_ly() method
text:

bp <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x = ~Old,
          y = ~Young,

bp

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
In this exercise, I practice the basic idea of Layered Grammar of Graphics using ggplot2.
By the end, I should be able to build common statistical charts by combining key components: data, aes(), geoms, stats, facets, coordinates, and themes.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Installing and loading the required libraries
text:
Installing and loading the required libraries
The code chunk below uses p_load() from pacman to check whether the packages are installed.
If not installed, it will install them; if installed, it will load them into the R session.

pacman::p_load(tidyverse)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Importing the data
Here we import the dataset Exam_data.csv using read_csv() (from readr, which is part of tidyverse).

exam_data <- read_csv("../data/Exam_data.csv")

Quick notes about the dataset:

It contains year-end exam results for a cohort of Primary 3 students.
Categorical variables: ID, CLASS, GENDER, RACE
Continuous variables: MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: R Graphics vs ggplot2 (a quick comparison)
text:
R Graphics vs ggplot2 (a quick comparison)
Below is a simple histogram using base R.

hist(exam_data$MATHS)








Now we do a similar plot using ggplot2.
Although the code is longer, ggplot2 is more flexible and scalable when plots become more complex.

ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=10,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="black",
                 fill="grey") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
1) data
Calling ggplot() with only data= gives a blank canvas (a ggplot object).

ggplot(data=exam_data)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
2) Aesthetic mappings: aes()
Here we add an aesthetic mapping (x-axis is MATHS).
Still no geom, so it will not draw points/bars yet.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x= MATHS))

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
3) Geoms (geometric objects)
A plot needs at least one geom. We add geoms with +.

geom_bar (bar chart for counts)

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()










geom_dotplot
A dotplot is useful to see a binned distribution using dots.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)








We can adjust binwidth and hide the y-axis:

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2.5,
               dotsize = 0.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,
                     breaks = NULL)










geom_histogram
Default histogram:

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()









Modifying a geom (change parameters in geom)

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,
                 color="black",
                 fill="light blue")










Modifying the mapping (change aes())
Here we map fill to GENDER so different genders get different fills.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x= MATHS,
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,
                 color="grey30")











geom_density
Density plot for a smooth distribution curve:

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()








If we add colour = GENDER, we will get separate curves by gender.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS,
           colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()










geom_boxplot
Boxplot is good for comparing distribution between groups.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS,
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot()








Notched boxplot:

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS,
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)










geom_violin
Violin plot shows the distribution shape.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS,
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_violin()










geom_point (scatterplot)

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x= MATHS,
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()










Combine geoms
Here we combine a boxplot with jittered points to show both summary + individual values.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS,
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(position="jitter",
             size = 0.5)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Working with stat()
A boxplot itself uses statistical transformation internally:

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
stat_summary() method (add mean)
We can add mean points using stat_summary().

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = "point",
               fun = "mean",
               colour ="red",
               size=4)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Using geom() but override stat
Same idea, but we use geom_point() with stat="summary":

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",
             fun="mean",
             colour="red",
             size=4)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Add a best fit curve on a scatterplot
text:
Add a best fit curve on a scatterplot
Default smooth:

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size=0.5)








We can specify a linear model explicitly:

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x= MATHS,
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm,
              linewidth=0.5)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
facet_wrap()

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
  facet_wrap(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
facet_grid()

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
  facet_grid(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
coord_flip()

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()








Flip the axes:

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Changing x/y range with coord_cartesian()
This is a “zoom” without dropping data outside the range.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm,
              size=0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

title: Hands-on Exercise 3_1
text: In this hands-on exercise, I learn how to create interactive data visualisation by using functions provided by ggiraph and plotly.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3_1
text:
3.4.1 Tooltip effect with tooltip aesthetic
The code chunk has two parts:
(1) create a ggplot object, (2) use girafe() to display it.

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL)

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6 * 0.618
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3_1
text:
3.5.1 Displaying multiple information on tooltip
Here I create a new field tooltip and include both ID and CLASS.

exam_data$tooltip <- c(paste0(
  "Name = ", exam_data$ID,
  "
 Class = ", exam_data$CLASS
))

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = exam_data$tooltip),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL)

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8 * 0.618
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3_1
text:
3.6.1 Customising Tooltip style
I use opts_tooltip() to customise tooltip rendering with css.

tooltip_css <- "background-color:white;
font-style:bold; color:black;"

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL)

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3_1
text:
3.6.2 Displaying statistics on tooltip
This example uses a function to compute the mean and +/- value (mean_se), then show it on tooltip.

tooltip <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean, "+/-", sem)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
                   aes(x = RACE)) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS,
        tooltip = after_stat(tooltip(y, ymax))),
    fun.data = "mean_se",
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill = "light blue"
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom = "errorbar",
    width = 0.2,
    linewidth = 0.3
  )

title: Hands-on Exercise 4A: Funnel Plots for Fair Comparisons
text:
In this hands-on exercise, I practise funnel plots for fair comparison across sub-districts.
I follow the structure in the teaching notes and create:

funnel plots using FunnelPlotR,
a static funnel plot built step-by-step with ggplot2, and
an interactive version using plotly.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4A: Funnel Plots for Fair Comparisons
text:
8.4.1 The basic plot

funnel_plot(
  .data = covid19,
  numerator = Positive,
  denominator = Death,
  group = `Sub-district`
)








A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 0 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4A: Funnel Plots for Fair Comparisons
text:
8.4.2 Makeover 1
Use data_type = "PR" to show proportions (Death / Positive), and zoom by xrange and yrange.

funnel_plot(

  numerator = Death,

text:
8.4.3 Makeover 2
Remove outlier labels and add titles.

funnel_plot(
  .data = covid19,
  numerator = Death,
  denominator = Positive,
  group = `Sub-district`,
  data_type = "PR",
  xrange = c(0, 6500),
  yrange = c(0, 0.05),
  label = NA,
  title = "Cumulative COVID-19 Fatality Rate by Cumulative Total Number of COVID-19 Positive Cases",
  x_label = "Cumulative COVID-19 Positive Cases",
  y_label = "Cumulative Fatality Rate"
)








A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 7 are outliers.
