title: Hands-on Exercise 4A: Funnel Plots for Fair Comparisons
text:
8.5.1 Computing the derived fields

df <- covid19 %>%
  mutate(rate = Death / Positive) %>%
  mutate(rate.se = sqrt((rate * (1 - rate)) / Positive)) %>%
  filter(rate > 0)

fit.mean <- weighted.mean(df$rate, 1 / df$rate.se^2)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4A: Funnel Plots for Fair Comparisons
text:
8.5.2 Compute 95% and 99.9% control limits

number.seq <- seq(1, max(df$Positive), 1)

number.ll95  <- fit.mean - 1.96 * sqrt((fit.mean * (1 - fit.mean)) / number.seq)
number.ul95  <- fit.mean + 1.96 * sqrt((fit.mean * (1 - fit.mean)) / number.seq)
number.ll999 <- fit.mean - 3.29 * sqrt((fit.mean * (1 - fit.mean)) / number.seq)
number.ul999 <- fit.mean + 3.29 * sqrt((fit.mean * (1 - fit.mean)) / number.seq)

dfCI <- data.frame(
  number.ll95, number.ul95, number.ll999, number.ul999,
  number.seq, fit.mean
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4A: Funnel Plots for Fair Comparisons
text:
8.5.3 Static funnel plot

p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = Positive, y = rate)) +
  geom_point(aes(label = `Sub-district`), alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_line(data = dfCI, aes(x = number.seq, y = number.ll95),
            size = 0.4, colour = "grey40", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(data = dfCI, aes(x = number.seq, y = number.ul95),
            size = 0.4, colour = "grey40", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(data = dfCI, aes(x = number.seq, y = number.ll999),
            size = 0.4, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_line(data = dfCI, aes(x = number.seq, y = number.ul999),
            size = 0.4, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_hline(data = dfCI, aes(yintercept = fit.mean),
             size = 0.4, colour = "grey40") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 0.05)) +
  annotate("text", x = 1,   y = -0.13, label = "95%", size = 3, colour = "grey40") +
  annotate("text", x = 4.5, y = -0.18, label = "99%", size = 3, colour = "grey40") +
  ggtitle("Cumulative Fatality Rate by Cumulative Number of COVID-19 Cases") +
  xlab("Cumulative Number of COVID-19 Cases") +
  ylab("Cumulative Fatality Rate") +
  theme_light() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.position = c(0.91, 0.85),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 7),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7),
    legend.background = element_rect(colour = "grey60", linetype = "dotted"),
    legend.key.height = unit(0.3, "cm")
  )

p

)

For correlation matrix, we focus on the numeric columns (usually columns 2 to 12).

title: Hands-on Exercise 06: Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
4 6.4 Building the correlation matrix: pairs()

4.1 6.4.1 Basic scatterplot matrix

pairs(wine_num)










4.2 6.4.2 Drawing only one triangle (lower / upper)
Lower triangle only:

pairs(wine_num, upper.panel = NULL)








Upper triangle only:

pairs(wine_num, lower.panel = NULL)










4.3 6.4.3 Add correlation coefficients
We replace one panel with a correlation-number panel.

panel.cor <- function(x, y, digits = 2, prefix = "", cex.cor, ...) {
  usr <- par("usr"); on.exit(par(usr))
  par(usr = c(0, 1, 0, 1))
  r <- abs(cor(x, y, use = "complete.obs"))
  txt <- format(c(r, 0.123456789), digits = digits)[1]
  txt <- paste0(prefix, txt)
  if (missing(cex.cor)) cex.cor <- 0.8 / strwidth(txt)
  text(0.5, 0.5, txt, cex = cex.cor * (1 + r) / 2)
}

pairs(wine_num, upper.panel = panel.cor)

title: Hands-on Exercise 06: Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
5 6.5 Visualising correlation matrix: ggcorrmat()
A clean, publication-style correlation plot.

ggstatsplot::ggcorrmat(
  data = wine,
  cor.vars = 1:11,               # adjust if your dataset has different columns
  ggcorrplot.args = list(outline.color = "black"),
  hc.order = TRUE,
  tl.cex = 10,
  title = "Correlogram for wine dataset",
  subtitle = "Pearson correlation (x = non-significant at p < 0.05; Holm adjustment)"
)









5.1 6.6 Building multiple plots (faceting by a group)
If you have a group variable like type (red/white), you can facet the correlogram.

wine <- wine %>%
  janitor::clean_names() %>%
  mutate(type = as.factor(type))

num_vars <- wine %>% select(where(is.numeric)) %>% names()
stopifnot(length(num_vars) >= 2)

wine_corr <- wine %>% select(type, all_of(num_vars))

ggstatsplot::grouped_ggcorrmat(
  data = wine_corr,
  cor.vars = num_vars,
  grouping.var = type,   # ✅ 注意这里！！
  type = "robust",
  p.adjust.method = "holm",
  plotgrid.args = list(ncol = 2),
  ggcorrplot.args = list(outline.color = "black"),
  hc.order = TRUE,
  tl.cex = 10,
  annotation.args = list(
    title = "Correlogram for wine dataset",
    caption = "Dataset: UCI Machine Learning Repository"
  )
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 06: Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
6 6.7 Visualising correlation matrix using corrplot

6.1 6.7.1 Getting started

wine_cor <- cor(wine_num, use = "complete.obs")
corrplot(wine_cor)










6.2 6.7.2 Visual geometries (ellipse)

corrplot(wine_cor, method = "ellipse")










6.3 6.7.3 Working with layout (lower)

corrplot(wine_cor, method = "ellipse", type = "lower")








You can customize diagonal and text color:

corrplot(wine_cor, method = "ellipse", type = "lower",
         diag = FALSE, tl.col = "black")










6.4 6.7.4 Mixed layout (numbers + shapes)

corrplot.mixed(wine_cor,
               lower = "ellipse",
               upper = "number",
               tl.pos = "lt",
               diag = "l",
               tl.col = "black")










6.5 6.7.5 Combine corrgram with significance test
Compute p-values using cor.test() pairwise, then pass into corrplot() via p.mat.

cor.mtest <- function(mat, conf.level = 0.95) {
  mat <- as.matrix(mat)
  n <- ncol(mat)
  p.mat <- matrix(NA, n, n)
  diag(p.mat) <- 0
  for (i in 1:(n - 1)) {
    for (j in (i + 1):n) {
      tmp <- cor.test(mat[, i], mat[, j], conf.level = conf.level)
      p.mat[i, j] <- p.mat[j, i] <- tmp$p.value
    }
  }
  colnames(p.mat) <- rownames(p.mat) <- colnames(mat)
  p.mat
}

wine_sig <- cor.mtest(wine_num)

corrplot(wine_cor,
         method = "number",
         type = "lower",
         diag = FALSE,
         tl.col = "black",
         tl.srt = 45,
         p.mat = wine_sig,
         sig.level = 0.05)










6.6 6.7.6 Reorder a corrgram

corrplot.mixed(wine_cor,
               lower = "ellipse",
               upper = "number",
               tl.pos = "lt",
               diag = "l",
               order = "AOE",
               tl.col = "black")










6.7 6.7.7 Reordering using hierarchical clustering (hclust)

corrplot(wine_cor,
         method = "ellipse",
         tl.pos = "lt",
         order = "hclust",
         hclust.method = "ward.D",
         addrect = 3)

If your dataset has missing values, use use = "complete.obs" in cor().
Always sanity-check: correlation ≠ causation.

title: Hands-on Exercise 14: Heatmap for Visualising and Analysing Multivariate Data
text:
5 14.5 Creating interactive heatmap (heatmaply)

5.1 14.5.1 Basic heatmaply

heatmaply(wh_matrix)







5.2 14.5.2 Data transformation options
Scaling (z-score) column-wise:

heatmaply(wh_matrix, scale = "column")





Normalising (0–1 scale) on the input data (typically after selecting numeric cols):

heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix))





Percentising (convert each value into percentile rank):

heatmaply(percentize(wh_matrix))







5.3 14.5.3 Clustering method & number of clusters
You can compare clustering methods using dendextend (one practical workflow is to compute distances and evaluate).
Below shows a commonly-used approach consistent with the lecture workflow: estimate the number of clusters (k) via silhouette.

# distance and clustering
wh_d <- dist(normalize(wh_matrix), method = "euclidean")
wh_clust <- hclust(wh_d, method = "average")

# find a reasonable k using silhouette (heatmaply provides helpers in some setups)
num_k <- find_k(wh_clust)
plot(num_k)








Then plot with a selected k (example: k = 3):

heatmaply(
  normalize(wh_matrix),
  dist_method = "euclidean",
  hclust_method = "average",
  k_row = 3
)







5.4 14.5.4 Seriation (re-ordering)
Seriation reorders rows/columns to make similar items adjacent. Try different methods:

heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix), seriate = "OLO")




heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix), seriate = "GW")




heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix), seriate = "mean")




heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix), seriate = "none")







5.5 14.5.5 Colour palettes
Use a single-hue palette (e.g., Blues) to avoid misleading diverging colors when all values are positive.

heatmaply(
  normalize(wh_matrix),
  seriate = "none",
  colors = viridisLite::viridis(256)
)







5.6 14.5.6 Finishing touch
Add clearer labels and tune margins/font sizes.

heatmaply(
  normalize(wh_matrix),
  seriate = "none",
  colors = viridisLite::viridis(256),
  k_row = 5,
  margins = c(50, 200, 60, 20),
  fontsize_row = 4,
  fontsize_col = 5,
  main = "World Happiness Score and Variables by Country, 2018",
  xlab = "World Happiness Indicators",
  ylab = "World Countries"
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 14: Heatmap for Visualising and Analysing Multivariate Data
text:
6 Notes / Tips

Heatmaps are very sensitive to scaling: always decide if you need raw vs scaled values.
If your row labels become unreadable, consider filtering top-N countries or using interactive mode.

text:
In this hands-on exercise, I replicate the workflow demonstrated in the lecture notes (Chap 16) to design treemaps using:

treemap (static treemaps)
treemapify (ggplot2-style treemaps)
d3treeR (interactive treemap, optional)

Note (dataset year): The lecture screenshots use a 2017 example, but the provided realis2018.csv contains 2018 transactions. I keep the same design steps and update titles to 2018 so that the plots match the data. fileciteturn1file0

Important: To avoid long renders and Windows compilation issues, do not install packages during render.
Install once in the Console if needed:

}

library(treemap)

3 16.3 Data Wrangling

3.1 16.3.1 Importing the dataset

realis2018 <- readr::read_csv("../data/realis2018.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
realis2018 <- realis2018 %>% janitor::clean_names()

3.2 16.3.2 Data wrangling and manipulation
The raw records are at transaction level (very granular). For treemaps, we summarise by a small set of geographic and property descriptors.
Goal: compute

Total units sold
Total area
Median unit price (psm)
Median transacted price

grouped by:

project_name
planning_region
planning_area
property_type
type_of_sale



3.3 16.3.3 Grouped summaries (without the pipe)

realis2018_grouped <- dplyr::group_by(
  realis2018,
  project_name, planning_region, planning_area, property_type, type_of_sale
)

realis2018_summarised <- dplyr::summarise(
  realis2018_grouped,
  total_units_sold = sum(no_of_units, na.rm = TRUE),
  total_area = sum(area_sqm, na.rm = TRUE),
  median_unit_price_psm = median(unit_price_psm, na.rm = TRUE),
  median_transacted_price = median(transacted_price, na.rm = TRUE),
  .groups = "drop"
)

Personally I find the pipe version easier to read because the steps flow top-to-bottom and I do not need to create intermediate objects unless I want to inspect them.

title: Hands-on Exercise 05e: Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
4 16.4 Designing Treemap with treemap package
In this section, I follow the lecture sequence to explore how index, vSize, vColor, type, palettes and layout parameters affect the treemap.

4.1 16.4.1 Selecting records for treemap design
To stay consistent with the lecture example, I focus on Condominium and Resale records. (This also reduces clutter so the main patterns are easier to see.)

realis2018_selected <- realis2018_summarised %>%
  filter(property_type == "Condominium", type_of_sale == "Resale") %>%
  filter(total_units_sold > 0)

realis2018_selected %>% slice_head(n = 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 9
   project_name         planning_region planning_area property_type type_of_sale
   <chr>                <chr>           <chr>         <chr>         <chr>       
 1 1 KING ALBERT PARK   Central Region  Bukit Timah   Condominium   Resale      
 2 3 ORCHARD BY-THE-PA… Central Region  Orchard       Condominium   Resale      
 3 368 THOMSON          Central Region  Novena        Condominium   Resale      
 4 6 DERBYSHIRE         Central Region  Novena        Condominium   Resale      
 5 8 @ MOUNT SOPHIA     Central Region  Rochor        Condominium   Resale      
 6 8 NAPIER             Central Region  Tanglin       Condominium   Resale      
 7 8 SAINT THOMAS       Central Region  River Valley  Condominium   Resale      
 8 8@WOODLEIGH          Central Region  Toa Payoh     Condominium   Resale      
 9 A TREASURE TROVE     North East Reg… Punggol       Condominium   Resale      
10 AALTO                Central Region  Marine Parade Condominium   Resale      
# ℹ 4 more variables: total_units_sold <dbl>, total_area <dbl>,
#   median_unit_price_psm <dbl>, median_transacted_price <dbl>




4.2 16.4.2 Using the basic arguments
This is the “starter” treemap: hierarchy = region → area → project; rectangle size = units sold; colour = median unit price.

treemap(
  realis2018_selected,
  index = c("planning_region", "planning_area", "project_name"),
  vSize = "total_units_sold",
  vColor = "median_unit_price_psm",
  title = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2018",
  title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
)










4.3 16.4.3 Working with vColor and type
In the lecture, the warning is that if we do not specify type, the default may treat the colour mapping differently. Setting type = "value" makes the legend behave more intuitively for numeric colour values.

treemap(
  realis2018_selected,
  index = c("planning_region", "planning_area", "project_name"),
  vSize = "total_units_sold",
  vColor = "median_unit_price_psm",
  type = "value",
  title = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2018",
  palette = "Greens",
  title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
)










4.4 16.4.4 Colours in treemap package

4.4.1 Diverging palette (may look confusing when all values are positive)

treemap(
  realis2018_selected,
  index = c("planning_region", "planning_area", "project_name"),
  vSize = "total_units_sold",
  vColor = "median_unit_price_psm",
  type = "value",
  palette = "RdYlBu",
  title = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2018",
  title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
)










4.4.2 Manual type
Manual type maps the value range linearly without binning. I use it to compare how the legend and colour spread differ.

treemap(
  realis2018_selected,
  index = c("planning_region", "planning_area", "project_name"),
  vSize = "total_units_sold",
  vColor = "median_unit_price_psm",
  type = "manual",
  palette = "Blues",
  title = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2018",
  title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
)











4.5 16.4.7 Treemap layout (algorithm)
The lecture compares squarified and pivotSize. Here I explicitly set algorithm = "squarified".

treemap(
  realis2018_selected,
  index = c("planning_region", "planning_area", "project_name"),
  vSize = "total_units_sold",
  vColor = "median_unit_price_psm",
  type = "manual",
  palette = "Blues",
  algorithm = "squarified",
  title = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2018",
  title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
)










4.6 16.4.9 Using sortID (with pivotSize)
With pivotSize, sortID controls the order of rectangle placement. Following the lecture, I sort by median_transacted_price.

treemap(
  realis2018_selected,
  index = c("planning_region", "planning_area", "project_name"),
  vSize = "total_units_sold",
  vColor = "median_unit_price_psm",
  type = "manual",
  palette = "Blues",
  algorithm = "pivotSize",
  sortID = "median_transacted_price",
  title = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2018",
  title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 05e: Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
5 16.5 Designing Treemap using treemapify package
treemapify is convenient when I want treemaps that behave like normal ggplot layers, so I can style them using themes and scales.

5.1 16.5.1 Designing a basic treemap

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area = total_units_sold,
           fill = median_unit_price_psm)) +
  geom_treemap() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "light blue", high = "blue") +
  labs(
    title = "Resale Condominium (Treemapify), 2018",
    fill = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()










5.2 16.5.2 Defining hierarchy

5.2.1 Group by Planning Region

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area = total_units_sold,
           fill = median_unit_price_psm,
           subgroup = planning_region)) +
  geom_treemap() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "light blue", high = "blue") +
  labs(
    title = "Treemapify with Hierarchy: Planning Region (2018)",
    fill = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()










5.2.2 Group by Planning Region + Planning Area

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area = total_units_sold,
           fill = median_unit_price_psm,
           subgroup = planning_region,
           subgroup2 = planning_area)) +
  geom_treemap() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "light blue", high = "blue") +
  labs(
    title = "Treemapify with Hierarchy: Region + Area (2018)",
    fill = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()










5.2.3 Adding boundary lines
This step makes the hierarchical blocks easier to read (especially when there are many small projects).

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area = total_units_sold,
           fill = median_unit_price_psm,
           subgroup = planning_region,
           subgroup2 = planning_area)) +
  geom_treemap() +
  geom_treemap_subgroup2_border(colour = "gray40", size = 2) +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_border(colour = "gray20") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "light blue", high = "blue") +
  labs(
    title = "Treemapify with Borders: Region + Area (2018)",
    fill = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on Exercise 05e: Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
6 16.6 Designing Interactive Treemap using d3treeR

6.1 16.6.1 Installing d3treeR (optional)
The lecture installs d3treeR from GitHub. On Windows, this can be slow if it triggers compilation.
If you want the interactive plot, install once in Console:

install.packages("remotes", type="binary")
remotes::install_github("timelyportfolio/d3treeR")



6.2 16.6.2 Designing an interactive treemap
This chunk will render the interactive treemap only if d3treeR is installed. Otherwise it will show a short message (and the document will still render).

if (requireNamespace("d3treeR", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(d3treeR)

  tm <- treemap(
    realis2018_selected,
    index = c("planning_region", "planning_area"),
    vSize = "total_units_sold",
    vColor = "median_unit_price_psm",
    type = "value",
    title = "Private Residential Property Sold, 2018",
    title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
  )

  d3treeR::d3tree(tm, rootname = "Singapore")
} else {
  cat("d3treeR is not installed. Install it in Console to view the interactive treemap.")
}

d3treeR is not installed. Install it in Console to view the interactive treemap.

title: Hands-on Exercise 8A: Choropleth Mapping with R
text: Choropleth mapping is used to visualise areal differences by shading polygons with attribute values. In this exercise, I follow the lecturer’s workflow to build choropleth maps for Singapore planning subzones. Compared with a raw default map, the later sections show how data classification, colour scheme and layout settings can improve readability.

3.3 21.3.3 Data preparation
To match the lecturer’s example, I prepare the year 2020 table and derive the following variables:

YOUNG: age group 0 to 24
ECONOMY ACTIVE: age group 25 to 64
AGED: age group 65 and above
TOTAL: total population
DEPENDENCY: (YOUNG + AGED) / ECONOMY ACTIVE



Code
popdata2020 <- popdata %>%
  filter(Time == 2020) %>%
  mutate(
    age_start = readr::parse_number(AG),
    age_group = case_when(
      age_start < 25 ~ "YOUNG",
      age_start < 65 ~ "ECONOMY ACTIVE",
      TRUE ~ "AGED"
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(PA, SZ, age_group) %>%
  summarise(POP = sum(Pop), .groups = "drop") %>%
  tidyr::complete(
    PA,
    SZ,
    age_group = c("YOUNG", "ECONOMY ACTIVE", "AGED"),
    fill = list(POP = 0)
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = age_group, values_from = POP) %>%
  mutate(
    TOTAL = YOUNG + `ECONOMY ACTIVE` + AGED,
    DEPENDENCY = (YOUNG + AGED) / `ECONOMY ACTIVE`
  ) %>%
  select(PA, SZ, YOUNG, `ECONOMY ACTIVE`, AGED, TOTAL, DEPENDENCY)

To make the join work correctly, I standardise the case of the planning area and subzone names first.

  mutate(
    PA = toupper(PA),

title: Hands-on Exercise 8A: Choropleth Mapping with R
text:
4 21.4 Choropleth Mapping using tmap

4.1 21.4.1 Quick choropleth map using qtm()


Code
tmap_mode("plot")
qtm(mpsz_pop2020, fill = "DEPENDENCY")









Observation: This first map is quick to produce, but the styling is still very basic. The legend and border treatment are good enough for exploration, but not yet presentation quality.


4.2 21.4.2 A cleaner choropleth map using map elements

PlotCode




Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.blues"
    ),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Dependency ratio")
  ) +
  tm_title("Distribution of Dependency Ratio by planning subzone") +
  tm_layout(frame = TRUE) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_compass(type = "8star", size = 2) +
  tm_grid(alpha = 0.2) +
  tm_credits(
    "Source: Planning sub-zone boundary from Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA)\nPopulation data from Department of Statistics DOS",
    position = c("left", "bottom")
  )













Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.blues"
    ),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Dependency ratio")
  ) +
  tm_title("Distribution of Dependency Ratio by planning subzone") +
  tm_layout(frame = TRUE) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_compass(type = "8star", size = 2) +
  tm_grid(alpha = 0.2) +
  tm_credits(
    "Source: Planning sub-zone boundary from Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA)\nPopulation data from Department of Statistics DOS",
    position = c("left", "bottom")
  )












Insight: This version looks much closer to a cartographic map. The title, legend, compass and credits make the output easier to interpret and more suitable for reporting.


4.3 21.4.3 Drawing a base map


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons()











4.4 21.4.4 Drawing choropleth map by tm_polygons()


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons("DEPENDENCY")











4.5 21.4.5 Separating fill and border


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_fill("DEPENDENCY") +
  tm_borders(lwd = 0.1, alpha = 0.5)









Observation: When borders are added separately, the subzone boundaries become much clearer, especially in the dense central region.


4.6 21.4.6 Data classification methods of tmap

4.6.1 21.4.6.1 Jenks classification


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(style = "jenks", n = 5)
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)











4.6.2 21.4.6.2 Equal classification


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(style = "equal", n = 5)
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)











4.6.3 21.4.6.3 Quantile classification


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(style = "quantile", n = 5)
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)









Student note: The quantile version spreads areas more evenly across classes, so visually it is easier to compare the subzones. In contrast, equal intervals tend to leave many observations in similar light shades when the distribution is uneven.


4.6.4 21.4.6.4 Fixed breaks
It is a good habit to check summary statistics before deciding custom breaks.


Code
summary(mpsz_pop2020$DEPENDENCY)


   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.    NA's 
 0.0000  0.6519  0.7025  0.7742  0.7645 19.0000      92 




Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    breaks = c(0, 0.60, 0.70, 0.80, 0.90, 1.00)
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)









Observation: With fixed breaks, the legend becomes more policy-friendly because the cut points are chosen deliberately rather than fully determined by the software.



4.7 21.4.7 Colour scheme

4.7.1 21.4.7.1 Green palette


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.greens"
    )
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)











4.7.2 21.4.7.2 Reversed green palette


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "-brewer.greens"
    )
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)









Student note: I personally prefer the blue palette here because it looks slightly cleaner. But the reversed palette is useful when I want darker colours to emphasise lower values instead of higher values.



4.8 21.4.8 Map layouts

4.8.1 21.4.8.1 Map legend and title


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "jenks",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.greens"
    ),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Dependency ratio")
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_title("Distribution of Dependency Ratio by planning subzone\n(Jenks classification)")











4.8.2 21.4.8.2 Map style


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_fill(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    style = "quantile",
    palette = "Greens"
  ) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_style("classic")











4.8.3 21.4.8.3 Cartographic furniture


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.blues"
    ),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Dependency ratio")
  ) +
  tm_title("Distribution of Dependency Ratio by planning subzone") +
  tm_layout(frame = TRUE) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_compass(type = "8star", size = 2) +
  tm_grid(alpha = 0.2) +
  tm_credits(
    "Source: Planning Sub-zone boundary from Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA)\nPopulation data from Department of Statistics DOS",
    position = c("left", "bottom")
  )












4.9 21.4.9 Drawing small multiple choropleth maps

4.9.1 21.4.9.1 Multiple values in one aesthetic argument


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_fill(c("YOUNG", "AGED"),
          style = "equal",
          palette = "Blues") +
  tm_layout(legend.position = c("right", "bottom")) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_style("white")











4.9.2 21.4.9.2 Multiple small maps using tm_polygons()


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    c("DEPENDENCY", "AGED"),
    style = c("equal", "quantile"),
    palette = list("Blues", "Greens")
  ) +
  tm_layout(legend.position = c("right", "bottom"))











4.9.3 21.4.9.3 Group-by variable using tm_facets()


Code
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_fill(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    style = "quantile",
    palette = "Blues",
    midpoint = NA
  ) +
  tm_facets(by = "REGION_N", free.coords = TRUE) +
  tm_layout(
    legend.show = FALSE,
    title.position = c("center", "center"),
    title.size = 1.0
  ) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5)









Final reflection: Across all versions, the same data can give different impressions depending on classification and layout. This exercise reminded me that choropleth mapping is not just about plotting values. It is also about choosing a design that supports a fair visual comparison.

title: Hands-on Exercise 8C: Analytical Mapping
text:
3 23.3 Basic Choropleth Mapping

3.1 23.3.1 Visualising distribution of total and functional water points


Code
p1 <- tm_shape(NGA_wp) +
  tm_fill("total_wp", style = "quantile", palette = "Blues") +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_layout(main.title = "Distribution of total water point by LGAs")

p2 <- tm_shape(NGA_wp) +
  tm_fill("wp_functional", style = "quantile", palette = "Blues") +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_layout(main.title = "Distribution of functional water point by LGAs")

tmap_arrange(p1, p2, nrow = 1)









Observation: The two maps look similar in some regions because areas with many total water points also tend to have many functional water points. However, count maps alone do not show how efficient or reliable the water-point system is.

title: Hands-on Exercise 8C: Analytical Mapping
text:
4 23.4 Choropleth Map for Rates

4.1 23.4.1 Deriving proportion of functional and non-functional water points


Code
NGA_wp <- NGA_wp %>%
  mutate(
    pct_functional = wp_functional / total_wp,
    pct_nonfunctional = wp_nonfunctional / total_wp
  )




4.2 23.4.2 Plotting rate map


Code
tm_shape(NGA_wp) +
  tm_fill("pct_functional", style = "fixed",
          breaks = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1),
          palette = "Blues") +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_layout(main.title = "Rate map of functional water point by LGAs")









Insight: This map is more meaningful than the count map because it standardises by total water points. Regions with fewer total points are no longer automatically shown as unimportant.

title: Hands-on Exercise 8C: Analytical Mapping
text:
5 23.5 Extreme Value Maps

5.1 23.5.1 Percentile map

5.1.1 23.5.1.1 Data preparation


Code
NGA_wp <- NGA_wp %>% drop_na()




Code
percent <- c(0, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 0.9, 0.99, 1)
var <- NGA_wp["pct_functional"] %>%
  st_set_geometry(NULL)

quantile(var[, 1], percent)


       0%        1%       10%       50%       90%       99%      100% 
0.0000000 0.0000000 0.2169811 0.4791667 0.8611111 1.0000000 1.0000000 


Student note: When variables are extracted from an sf object, the geometry comes along as well. That is why st_set_geometry(NULL) is necessary here before using base functions like quantile().


5.1.2 23.5.1.2 Creating get.var() function


Code
get.var <- function(vname, df) {
  v <- df[vname] %>%
    st_set_geometry(NULL)
  v <- unname(v[, 1])
  return(v)
}




5.1.3 23.5.1.3 Percentile mapping function


Code
percentmap <- function(vnam, df, legtitle = NA, mtitle = "Percentile Map") {
  percent <- c(0, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 0.9, 0.99, 1)
  var <- get.var(vnam, df)
  bperc <- quantile(var, percent)

  tm_shape(df) +
    tm_polygons() +
    tm_shape(df) +
    tm_fill(
      vnam,
      title = legtitle,
      breaks = bperc,
      palette = "Blues",
      labels = c("< 1%", "1% - 10%", "10% - 50%", "50% - 90%", "90% - 99%", "> 99%")
    ) +
    tm_borders() +
    tm_layout(
      main.title = mtitle,
      legend.position = c("right", "bottom")
    )
}




5.1.4 23.5.1.4 Test the percentile mapping function


Code
percentmap("total_wp", NGA_wp)









Observation: The percentile map highlights the extreme ends of the distribution more clearly than a normal quantile map. It is useful when I specifically want to identify unusually low or unusually high areas.



5.2 23.5.2 Box map
First, I use a boxplot to understand the distribution of the non-functional water points.


Code
ggplot(data = NGA_wp,
       aes(x = "", y = wp_nonfunctional)) +
  geom_boxplot()









Observation: There are clear upper outliers, so a box map can help carry this boxplot logic into space.

5.2.1 23.5.2.1 Creating boxbreaks() function


Code
boxbreaks <- function(v, mult = 1.5) {
  qv <- unname(quantile(v))
  iqr <- qv[4] - qv[2]
  upfence <- qv[4] + mult * iqr
  lofence <- qv[2] - mult * iqr

  bb <- vector(mode = "numeric", length = 7)

  if (lofence < qv[1]) {
    bb[1] <- lofence
    bb[2] <- floor(qv[1])
  } else {
    bb[2] <- lofence
    bb[1] <- qv[1]
  }

  if (upfence > qv[5]) {
    bb[7] <- upfence
    bb[6] <- ceiling(qv[5])
  } else {
    bb[6] <- upfence
    bb[7] <- qv[5]
  }

  bb[3:5] <- qv[2:4]
  return(bb)
}




5.2.2 23.5.2.2 Reusing get.var() and testing the breaks


Code
var <- get.var("wp_nonfunctional", NGA_wp)
boxbreaks(var)


[1] -56.5   0.0  14.0  34.0  61.0 131.5 278.0




5.2.3 23.5.2.3 Box map function


Code
boxmap <- function(vnam, df, legtitle = NA, mtitle = "Box Map", mult = 1.5) {
  var <- get.var(vnam, df)
  bb <- boxbreaks(var)

  tm_shape(df) +
    tm_polygons() +
    tm_shape(df) +
    tm_fill(
      vnam,
      title = legtitle,
      breaks = bb,
      palette = "Blues",
      labels = c("lower outlier",
                 "< 25%",
                 "25% - 50%",
                 "50% - 75%",
                 "> 75%",
                 "upper outlier")
    ) +
    tm_borders() +
    tm_layout(
      main.title = mtitle,
      title.position = c("left", "top")
    )
}




5.2.4 23.5.2.4 Test the box map function


Code
tmap_mode("plot")
boxmap("wp_nonfunctional", NGA_wp)









Insight: The box map is useful because it keeps the quartile structure while also flagging extreme observations explicitly. In my view, this makes it more interpretable than a standard choropleth when the purpose is outlier detection.

title: Hands-on Exercise 8C: Analytical Mapping
text:
6 23.6 Final reflection
This chapter helped me understand why analytical mapping is not only about aesthetics. It is mainly about choosing a mapping logic that matches the question. If I care about relative performance, I should use rates. If I care about outliers, percentile maps and box maps are more informative than standard classed choropleths.

title: ISSS608-VAA
text: Welcome to ISSS608 Visual Analytics and Applications. In this website,you will find my coursework prepared for this course.

title: Take-Home Exercise 2: Confirmatory Data Analysis and UI Design
text:
This page continues the earlier exploratory work and focuses on confirmatory data analysis (CDA) for the Colombian fintech dataset. Instead of scanning the full dataset again, this module tests a smaller set of targeted questions derived from the EDA findings. The goal is to check whether the key behavioural patterns remain visible when the analysis is narrowed to specific group comparisons and selected business indicators.
The second part of this page presents the UI design for the proposed EDA+CDA Shiny module. This section shows how the visualisations in this report can be translated into an interactive dashboard layout for users to explore customer profile, transaction behaviour, and comparison results more efficiently.

title: Take-Home Exercise 2: Confirmatory Data Analysis and UI Design
section: 3 Loading and preparing the analytical dataset
text:
3 Loading and preparing the analytical dataset


Code
customer_data <- readr::read_csv("../data/customer_data.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  janitor::clean_names()

transactions_data <- readr::read_csv("../data/transactions_data.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  janitor::clean_names() %>%
  mutate(date = as.Date(date))

mode_value <- function(x) {
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]
  if (length(x) == 0) return(NA_character_)
  names(sort(table(x), decreasing = TRUE))[1]
}

tx_summary <- transactions_data %>%
  group_by(customer_id) %>%
  summarise(
    tx_total_count = n(),
    tx_avg_amount = mean(amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    tx_total_amount = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    tx_type_diversity = n_distinct(type),
    dominant_tx_type = mode_value(type),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

df <- customer_data %>%
  left_join(tx_summary, by = "customer_id") %>%
  mutate(
    city = stringr::str_trim(stringr::str_extract(location, "^[^,]+")),
    log_tx_total_amount = log1p(tx_total_amount),
    log_tx_total_count = log1p(tx_total_count),
    log_clv = log1p(customer_lifetime_value)
  )

title: Take-Home Exercise 2: Confirmatory Data Analysis and UI Design
text:
4 Confirmatory Data Analysis

4.1 Analytical focus
Based on the earlier EDA results, I focus on four targeted questions:

Do higher-income customers generate larger total transaction amounts?
Are customer segments behaviourally different in activity intensity and transaction diversity?
Is stronger app engagement associated with lower churn probability?
Do customers with more active products have higher lifetime value?

These are not meant to be final causal claims. Instead, they are focused checks that help narrow the story of the dataset before the team moves into clustering and predictive modelling.


4.2 1. Income bracket and total transaction amount


Code
kw_income <- kruskal.test(tx_total_amount ~ income_bracket, data = df)

ggplot(df, aes(x = income_bracket, y = log1p(tx_total_amount), fill = income_bracket)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.15, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title = "Total transaction amount by income bracket",
    subtitle = paste0("Kruskal-Wallis p-value: ", signif(kw_income$p.value, 4)),
    x = NULL,
    y = "log(1 + total transaction amount)"
  )









Observation: Higher income groups generally show higher transaction amount distributions, while the low and medium income groups are concentrated at lower levels.
Insight: The difference is not only visible in raw spending level but also still visible after log transformation. This supports the idea that income bracket is an important explanatory factor for transaction volume in this dataset.


4.3 2. Customer segment and behavioural intensity


Code
segment_summary <- df %>%
  group_by(customer_segment) %>%
  summarise(
    median_tx_count = median(tx_total_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_type_diversity = median(tx_type_diversity, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_tx_amount = median(tx_total_amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

p_seg_count <- ggplot(segment_summary, aes(x = reorder(customer_segment, median_tx_count), y = median_tx_count, fill = customer_segment)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  labs(
    title = "Median transaction count by segment",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Median count"
  )

p_seg_div <- ggplot(segment_summary, aes(x = reorder(customer_segment, median_type_diversity), y = median_type_diversity, fill = customer_segment)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  labs(
    title = "Median transaction type diversity by segment",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Median number of types"
  )

p_seg_count + p_seg_div









Observation: Power and regular customers clearly outperform inactive and occasional customers in both transaction frequency and behavioural breadth.
Insight: This result gives a strong business interpretation to the predefined customer segments. They are not only labels in the data table; they also represent meaningful differences in how customers use the platform.


4.4 3. App engagement and churn probability


Code
engagement_cda <- df %>%
  mutate(login_decile = ntile(app_logins_frequency, 10)) %>%
  group_by(login_decile) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_login_frequency = mean(app_logins_frequency, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_churn_probability = mean(churn_probability, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cor_test <- cor.test(
  ~ app_logins_frequency + churn_probability,
  data = df,
  method = "spearman",
  use = "complete.obs"
)

ggplot(engagement_cda, aes(x = avg_login_frequency, y = avg_churn_probability)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, color = "#1F78B4") +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, color = "#1F78B4") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed", color = "#E31A1C") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Average churn probability across app engagement deciles",
    subtitle = paste0("Spearman rho = ", round(cor_test$estimate, 3), 
                      ", p-value = ", signif(cor_test$p.value, 4)),
    x = "Average app login frequency",
    y = "Average churn probability"
  )









Observation: Customers with stronger app engagement tend to have lower average churn probability, and the decline is fairly consistent across the engagement deciles.
Insight: This is one of the clearest confirmatory patterns in the module. It suggests that digital engagement is closely related to retention, which is likely to be useful for the team’s later predictive work.


4.5 4. Active products and customer lifetime value


Code
ggplot(df, aes(x = factor(active_products), y = log1p(customer_lifetime_value), fill = factor(active_products))) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.15, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues") +
  labs(
    title = "Customer lifetime value by number of active products",
    x = "Number of active products",
    y = "log(1 + customer lifetime value)"
  )









Observation: Customers with more active products tend to have a higher lifetime value distribution, especially when moving from one or two products to four or five products.
Insight: This pattern supports a cross-sell interpretation. Multi-product customers appear more valuable for the business, so product adoption depth is an important behaviour dimension rather than a minor profile variable.


4.6 5. Top-city comparison on transaction amount and churn


Code
top_city_cda <- df %>%
  count(city, sort = TRUE) %>%
  slice_head(n = 8) %>%
  select(city) %>%
  left_join(
    df %>%
      group_by(city) %>%
      summarise(
        median_tx_total_amount = median(tx_total_amount, na.rm = TRUE),
        avg_churn_probability = mean(churn_probability, na.rm = TRUE),
        .groups = "drop"
      ),
    by = "city"
  )

p_city_amount <- ggplot(top_city_cda, aes(x = reorder(city, median_tx_total_amount), y = median_tx_total_amount, fill = median_tx_total_amount)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#C7E9C0", high = "#238B45") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  labs(
    title = "Median transaction amount in top cities",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Median amount (COP)"
  )

p_city_churn <- ggplot(top_city_cda, aes(x = reorder(city, avg_churn_probability), y = avg_churn_probability, fill = avg_churn_probability)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#DEEBF7", high = "#2171B5") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Average churn probability in top cities",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Average churn probability"
  )

p_city_amount + p_city_churn









Observation: The top cities do not look identical. Some cities show stronger spending while others show slightly higher churn risk.
Insight: Location still matters even after narrowing the analysis to the largest cities. This means that geography can remain a meaningful filter in the future dashboard design.


4.7 6. Behavioural correlation heatmap


Code
corr_vars <- df %>%
  select(
    app_logins_frequency, feature_usage_diversity, active_products,
    tx_total_count, tx_total_amount, weekend_transaction_ratio,
    churn_probability, customer_lifetime_value
  )

corr_mat <- cor(corr_vars, use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "spearman")

corr_long <- as.data.frame(as.table(corr_mat)) %>%
  rename(var1 = Var1, var2 = Var2, corr = Freq)

ggplot(corr_long, aes(x = var1, y = var2, fill = corr)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(corr, 2)), size = 3) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#B2182B", mid = "white", high = "#2166AC", midpoint = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Spearman correlation heatmap of selected behavioural variables",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    fill = "Correlation"
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1))









Observation: Transaction count, transaction amount, active products, and lifetime value tend to move together, while churn probability is negatively related to engagement and value-oriented variables.
Insight: The heatmap gives a compact summary of the confirmatory story in this module. The main signals are internally consistent and support the use of behavioural variables in the later clustering and decision tree modules.

title: Take-Home Exercise 2: Confirmatory Data Analysis and UI Design
text:
5 UI Design

5.1 UI design overview
The proposed Shiny interface for this module is designed specifically for the EDA + CDA workflow rather than for the full team project. The aim is to let a user move from broad exploration to focused comparison in one dashboard, without forcing them to manually edit code.
The design follows a standard sidebar + main panel dashboard layout. The sidebar is used for filtering and variable selection, while the main panel is used for KPI cards, charts, and the detailed comparison tabs.



5.1.1 Input components

City selector
Gender selector
Income bracket selector
Customer segment selector
Transaction metric selector
Date range selector
Toggle for log scale
Button to reset filters




5.1.2 Output components

KPI cards
Distribution charts
Group comparison charts
Correlation heatmap
Interactive customer table
Short analytical notes
Downloadable filtered table






5.2 Storyboard


Code
flowchart LR
    A[Sidebar Filters<br/>City / Gender / Income / Segment / Metric / Date] --> B[Overview Tab<br/>KPI cards + key distributions]
    A --> C[EDA Tab<br/>distribution charts + monthly activity]
    A --> D[CDA Tab<br/>group comparisons + behavioural heatmap]
    A --> E[Data Table Tab<br/>filtered customer-level table]





flowchart LR
    A[Sidebar Filters<br/>City / Gender / Income / Segment / Metric / Date] --> B[Overview Tab<br/>KPI cards + key distributions]
    A --> C[EDA Tab<br/>distribution charts + monthly activity]
    A --> D[CDA Tab<br/>group comparisons + behavioural heatmap]
    A --> E[Data Table Tab<br/>filtered customer-level table]






A more detailed page-level storyboard is shown below.

+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Sidebar Filters |                         Main Dashboard Panel                          |
|-----------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| City            | KPI Cards: Customers | Median Tx Count | Median Tx Amount | Avg Churn |
| Gender          |-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Income          | Tab 1: Overview -> distributions and monthly activity                 |
| Segment         | Tab 2: CDA -> group comparison charts and correlation heatmap         |
| Metric          | Tab 3: Data Table -> filtered customer-level records                  |
| Date Range      |-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Log Scale       | Dynamic explanation box with short observations and insights           |
| Reset Button    |                                                                       |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

Observation: The storyboard keeps the control panel simple and puts most of the screen space into the analytical outputs.
Insight: This layout is practical because users usually spend less time choosing filters and more time reading charts. A wide main panel makes the comparison plots easier to interpret, especially when the page contains boxplots, bar charts, and heatmaps.


5.3 Description of UI components








Component
Purpose
Why it is useful




Sidebar filters
Allow users to select city, customer segment, income bracket, gender, and date range
These filters support both broad exploration and focused comparison


KPI cards
Show quick summary values after filtering
They help users understand the filtered subset before reading the charts


Overview tab
Display distributions and activity patterns
This preserves the exploratory nature of the first stage


CDA tab
Display confirmatory comparison charts and correlation heatmap
It helps users move from broad patterns to more targeted checks


Data table tab
Show the filtered customer-level table
It supports transparency and makes the visual results easier to verify


Log scale toggle
Switch between raw and log-transformed financial views
Financial variables are highly skewed, so this improves readability





5.4 Design principles used
The proposed interface follows several simple visual analytics principles.

Clarity first: only a small number of filters are placed in the sidebar, so the layout does not feel crowded.
Progressive analysis: users can move from overview charts to focused comparison charts in separate tabs.
Consistent colour usage: segment and group colours stay stable across charts to reduce confusion.
Readable scales: financial charts should allow a log-scale option because of right-skewed amount variables.
Interpretability: short text notes should be displayed beside important outputs so the dashboard remains understandable for non-technical users.

title: Take-Home Exercise 2: Confirmatory Data Analysis and UI Design
text:
6 Conclusion
This page narrowed the analysis into a set of focused confirmatory questions and showed that several important behavioural patterns remain strong: higher-income customers tend to transact more in total value, stronger customer segments show greater activity intensity, higher app engagement is associated with lower churn probability, and multi-product users tend to have higher lifetime value.
The page also proposed a Shiny dashboard design for the EDA+CDA module. The design is intentionally simple and filter-driven so that users can move from broad exploration to focused comparison in a single interface. This prototype can later be combined with the clustering and decision tree modules in the final team project.
